observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that Management require: 1) The Inventory Committee and the CGSO to prioritize the reconciliation of the RPCPPE with the accounting records after conducting the physical count, and pinpoint persons responsible for the missing properties who should be made to pay the value thereof as shown in the accounting records; 2) The CGSO to coordinate with the Accounting Office to fully account for the CY 2018-2022 PPE purchases, complete with data on the property tag, ARE, accountable officer, and office/location of the equipment, otherwise, those that cannot be accounted for shall be a personal liability of the person accountable for the said property; 3) The Inventory Committee to conduct the required semi-annual physical count of inventories in compliance with Section 124 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I; and 4) The CGSO to submit the SSMI with complete documentation to the Accounting Office as basis for preparing the journal entries to recognize the corresponding expense for the inventories consumed or distributed.
observations.managementAction: Continuous recommendations that reconciliation of GSO documents and inventories with the records of the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the GSO prepare weekly the SSMI duly supported with the RIS and other records and submit it to the Accounting Office as the basis for recognizing the corresponding expense in the books. We likewise recommended that the GSO and Accounting Office maintain stock cards and ledger cards, respectively, for each type of inventory to facilitate reconciliation with the actual stocks on hand.
observations.managementAction: The GSO had already started submitting SSMI to the Accounting Office and filling vacant positions. As of this date, although still subject for checking, two personnel already submitted the Distribution/Utilization Sheets of some/all supplies/equipment they received.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the General Services Office fully account for the fuel purchases based on valid supporting documents, reconciling these with the actual balance of fuel in the storage tank, to pinpoint responsibility for those that cannot be accounted for, and to refrain from making additional purchases until the balance is fully accounted for; Management justify the necessity and reasonableness of the quantities of fuel purchased, including the seven fuel procurements in December 2022, and why these should not be considered excessive, given the huge balance of inventory existing at year-end; The GSO and Accounting Office invariably prepare and maintain the records required under COA Circular No. 77-61, and to comply with all the requirements therein, including the regular submission of the consumption reports with complete supporting documents; The personnel in charge of monitoring the gasoline storage tank submit a written explanation of why fuel was released based on an unsigned RIS; The Internal Control Unit conduct spot checks to reconcile the remaining fuel tank balance with the GSO records. It is likewise imperative that the City establish a cut-off point to determine the level of fuel in the tank and then accounts for current purchases while working on the accountability for the prior purchases; and Management ensure that mitigating or compensating controls like periodic reconciliation of records and inventories, restricting fuel release based on a duly approved RIS, and updated recording and consumption monitoring are working effectively to reduce the risk of error or irregularities.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Office fully account for the fuel purchases based on valid supporting documents and reconciled and submitted to the Accounting Office. Submitted SSMI to Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Agriculturist and the City Veterinarian to prepare the Updated Schedule of Biological Assets of the City at the end of each reporting period and submit the same to the City Accounting Office for the immediate recognition of adjustments in the City’s books of accounts. The schedule should reflect the fair value less costs to sell for each biological asset (especially for breeding stocks), any additions resulting from birth, and reductions due to deaths and distributions. We further recommended that the City Inventory Committee conduct a physical count of all the biological assets recorded in the books of accounts of the City and that the GSO and the Accountant maintain the property cards and WOABSLC, respectively, and reconcile their records.
observations.managementAction: None

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Agriculturist and the City Veterinarian to prepare the Updated Schedule of Biological Assets of the City at the end of each reporting period and submit the same to the City Accounting Office for the immediate recognition of adjustments in the City’s books of accounts. The schedule should reflect the fair value less costs to sell for each biological asset (especially for breeding stocks), any additions resulting from birth, and reductions due to deaths and distributions. We further recommended that the City Inventory Committee conduct a physical count of all the biological assets recorded in the books of accounts of the City and that the GSO and the Accountant maintain the property cards and WOABSLC, respectively, and reconcile their records. We also recommended that the City Mayor ensure the conduct of the annual physical count of all biological assets owned by the City.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee and the CGSO to prioritize the reconciliation of the RPCPPE with the accounting records; CGSO to coordinate with the Accounting Office to fully account for the CY 2018-2022 PPE purchases; The Inventory Committee to conduct the required semi-annual physical count of inventories in compliance with Section 124 of the NGAS Manual; The CGSO to submit the SSMI with complete documentation to the Accounting Office as basis for preparing the journal entries to recognize the corresponding expense for the inventories consumed or distributed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Local Finance Committee to process the immediate return of the unutilized PDAF funds of ₱555,017.50 to the National Treasury, through the Department of Finance, in compliance with the Supreme Court Decision. Otherwise, the payment shall be a personal liability of the officials who authorized the said irregular disbursement.
observations.managementAction: Necessary actions are being done to immediately return the unutilized PDAF funds of ₱555,017.50 to the National Treasury through the Department of Finance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that, henceforth, Management closely monitor invariable compliance to the submission of monthly and quarterly financial reports with complete schedules and monthly collection and disbursement accounts in accordance with the requirement of pertinent laws and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant apologized for the delayed submission, though there was no intention to commit such lapses. The recommendation is well-taken and, in order to improve the submission of accounts, the City Accounting Office will procure a scanner to store electronic copies of the documents. They will do their best to submit the CY 2024 FS before the deadline.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management clearly delineate the functions of the City Budget Office which shall assume full responsibility for certifying the existence of available appropriations and in preparing and maintaining the required RAAOs per responsibility center as required under COA Circular No. 2006-002, and direct both the CBO and the CAO to reconcile their records at the end of each month and at year-end in accordance with NGAS.
observations.managementAction: The City Budget Office has already notified the City Accounting Office; however, the same has not been turned over since the latter also insists on preparing the same for the reason of ease in consolidating and reconciling the financial records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly implement the required deduction for existing loans for those with new applications/other payroll loans and to register their certifications for disapproval of new loan applications/other payroll loans as warranted under the circumstances.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office asserted that they will continue the registration of certifications for those with new loan applications, but fear that the employee may not be able to settle his/her obligation. However, they will not issue certifications for those with new loans and whose net take-home pay is below ₱5,000.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the LDRRM Council to properly plan the programs, projects, and activities to be undertaken charged to the correct fund source and ensure that the QRF is reserved for relief and recovery programs to address the immediate and short-term needs of its people during calamities. We further recommended that the LDRRM Council justify charging the said project to the QRF for further evaluation in audit.
observations.managementAction: The City Budget Officer will give necessary actions in coordination with the LDRRMO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City, through the LDRRMO, prioritize the finalization of the PSCP pursuant to NDRRMC Memorandum No. 33, s. 2018 immediately after the scheduled training, especially since it is way past the October 2021 deadline under CSC MC No. 12, s. 2021. We further recommended that the City Mayor institutionalize the PSCP by creating a working group that will perform the following activities: (a) Conduct a risk assessment; (b) Identify critical processes and functions; (c) Determine scenarios that may disrupt normal operation; (d) Conduct risk analysis and impact analysis; (e) Formulate the PSCP based on the identified risks on critical processes and functions and the related steps to be followed to eliminate, if not mitigate, the impact of the determined disruptions following the prescribed templates and requirements in the PSCP guidebook; and (f) Submit the plan to the CDRRMO for review, to the City Mayor for approval, and to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the passage of a resolution to institutionalize the PSCP.
observations.managementAction: Due to the cancelled schedule of training, the LDRRMO opted to facilitate by themselves, instead, the finalization of the PSCP by presenting the draft plan and using the PSCP guidebook, as well as the inputs from the online training course though Zoom and Blended Learning in October 2023 conducted by the Regional Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council through the Office of Civil Defense which will be scheduled not later than the first week of December, 2023 after we have completed our slides for the presentation as well as the forms to be printed. However, the scheduled activity for the first week was not pushed through. We have scheduled the same for facilitation by the accredited agency which is the OCD. We were just advised to send another letter requesting for the seminar-workshop from OCD scheduled for the first quarter of 2024 due to a change of leadership. The draft of the PSCP is included herein.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to compute the depreciation allowance of all its undepreciated Infrastructure Asset accounts for calendar year 2021 and backwards. Prior years' depreciation should be taken up as Prior Period Adjustment in the books to be closed to Government Equity at the end of the reporting period.
observations.managementAction: Depreciation allowance of all undepreciated Infrastructure Asset accounts (except Road Networks in the General Fund) for the calendar year 2021 and backwards had been computed and taken up under JEV No. 100-22-06-0047(3) dated 6/30/2022 which was submitted to the COA Bais through a Transmittal Letter dated October 25, 2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the City Accounting, Engineering and General Services Offices to strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines on local roads asset management system, and to earnestly perform their respective duties and responsibilities set forth under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015. Henceforth, we recommended that the Inventory Committee of the City conduct the annual physical count of all its Local Road Networks in accordance with regulations, making sure to produce the details necessary to be reported separately in the RPCLRN for submission to the Auditor and Accounting Office not later than January 31 of each year. The RPCLRN shall be reconciled with the accounting records and the necessary adjusting entries be prepared before the statement date.
observations.managementAction: In reference to the audit observation, a request to the City Engineering Office for the complete description and cost segregation of road components for each road project along with their corresponding useful life spans thru a Request Letter dated 6/27/2022 with attached copy of the Schedule of PPE as of December 31, 2021 was submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant review the transactions affecting the Due to/from Other Funds accounts, including that of prior years, and effect adjustments to reconcile the reciprocal account balances and present reliable data on the City's financial position as of a given period of time.
observations.managementAction: Adjustments were made through GF Journal Entry Voucher No. JEV 100-22-03-019 dated 3/31/2022, SEF Journal Entry Voucher No. GJ 200-22-09-017 dated 9/21/2022, and SEF Journal Entry Voucher No. GJ 200-22-09-018 dated 9/21/2022 which were all submitted to the COA Bais through a Transmittal Letter dated October 26, 2022. Additional compliance was also made through TF Journal Entry Voucher No. CK 300-22-11-069 dated 11/4/2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management substantiate the propriety of the procurement of the food items totaling ₱55,362,150.00 charged to the Bayanihan Grant to Cities and Municipalities (BGCM) distributed to the households, providing written explanations/justifications on each of the issues discussed above, and submit the following: (i) Daily menu for meals and the persons to whom these were served to the Audit Team for validation, encapsulated in SSMIs supported with the RIS which shall serve as basis for the Accountant in recognizing the expenses in the books; (ii) the Official Receipts/Sales Invoice to complement the Collection Receipts issued; (iii) Explanation for the absence of data in the labor component portion, except 'LGU'; (iv) Differences between the total quantities of the goods distributed as reflected in the submitted Summary of BGCM Relief Goods Distribution to 24 Barangays by the CSWDO and the Relief Goods Distribution Sheets prepared by Barangay Health Workers on the 14 barangays; (v) Differences between the reported quantity of relief goods received by the recipients and the actual quantity received by the beneficiaries as validated through the duly complied COA confirmation letters by the sample population of beneficiaries, and the other individual deficiencies noted above; (vi) Duplication of names and signatures that do not match; and (vii) Should the revalidation prove that the listed beneficiaries are indeed ineligible, require the responsible City Officials to refund the ESP given to the ineligible Job Order workers subject to any adjustments as a result of the CSWDO and HRMO validation, without prejudice to the demand for refund from the said beneficiaries as a matter of personal recourse.
observations.managementAction: The CSWDO had started submitting proof of distribution and the SSMIs to the Accounting Office; however, other documents are still being validated and compiled for submission to COA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit DOT Certificates of Authority to Operate of the hotels engaged by the City as quarantine facilities, provide the certified list of persons with complete information who were accommodated in these hotels and justify the need to incur additional costs for housing these persons in private hotels instead of government facilities.
observations.managementAction: None

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management desist from charging the purchase of motor vehicles and administrative costs to the LDRRMF and to reimburse the government for the said charges totaling ₱9,190,836.00. We further recommended that the City Budget Officer ensure that the LDRRMF Investment Plan passed by the CDRRMC thru the City Development Council as adapted in the Annual Investment Plan is compliant with the pertinent regulations to avoid inappropriate usages of the said fund, and henceforth, strictly observe the provisions of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1, DILG MC No. 2012-73 and other pertinent rules and regulations on the proper utilization of the LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRM Officer admitted that there is a need for reprogramming and the present administration is still on a tight budget. He recommended to coordinate with City Budget Office to make the necessary reallocation and reimbursement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City exhaust all means to implement without further delay the projects outlined under the 70% Mitigation Fund of the LDRRMF. It is also recommended that the unexpended balances of prior years' LDRRMF held in special trust be utilized upon due compliance with Section 6 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 in order to increase their constituents’ resilience and decrease their vulnerabilities to disasters and calamities.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO admitted that some funds were not yet utilized and substantive actions are being made to coordinate with the City Engineer to properly implement the projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant and the City Treasurer prioritize the review of these recorded payables to ascertain if these still represent valid claims to the extent possible, otherwise seek authority from the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the City Mayor to request for write-off of the account balances from the COA duly supported by the requirements stipulated under Section III.A.10 of COA Circular No. 97-001 so that these may be made available for valuable development programs and other priority activities of the City and so that the liabilities would be fairly presented in the financial statements. We further recommended that the City Accountant assign additional personnel to work on the preparation of the aging schedules and other important details of the four liability accounts earlier discussed so that these would be properly kept and presented in the financial statements to aid users thereof in decision-making.
observations.managementAction: Records are being reviewed to take up the appropriate journal entries. However, no final report has been prepared.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require CONEQUIP PHILIPPINES INC. to submit the warranty security in the required form on the awarded contract of the Procurement of Brand New Heavy Equipment and Vehicles totaling ₱137,389,164.00, and subsequently submit the other documents as prescribed under DILG Memorandum Circular (MC) No. 2014-155 as amended, as well as the other documentary requirements discussed above. Lastly, we recommended that Management submit justification as regards the provision of Administrative Order No. 227 and the importing of heavy equipment instead of sourcing it from domestic suppliers.
observations.managementAction: None

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit a written explanation why payments from the LCs were issued to Big Yellow instead of CONEQUIP and why payments were made against the LCs despite the lack of delivery of goods. We further recommended that Management submit the electronic fund transfers and, for the first five releases of the LCs, the corresponding promissory notes and the sales invoice be submitted to the Office of the Auditor for further verification.
observations.managementAction: A request letter for reimbursement dated June 23, 2021 was sent to the Board of Directors of the supplier CONEQUIP PHILIPPINES INC. (No refund received yet from the supplier); necessary adjusting entries were already made per issuance of JEV 100-21-06-0031 dated 6/25/2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC comply with the mandatory posting of the updated APP, NOA, and other information relative to the Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act in the GPPB Online Portal as required in GPPB Circular No. 01-2020. Henceforth, we recommended that the BAC should always be updated with the issuances and advisories of the GPPB by visiting their website regularly to avoid any more non-compliance.
observations.managementAction: None

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management substantiate the propriety of the procurement of the food items totaling ₱55,362,150.00 charged to the BGCM distributed to the households, providing written explanations/justifications on each of the issues discussed above, and submit the needed documentary evidence to each of the issues.
observations.managementAction: None

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the CSWDO revalidate the ineligibility of the 417 Job Order workers and that the HRMO review the remaining 1,089 names of other Job Order workers who may have been paid but were not included in the validated list. We further recommended that the CSWDO submit the evaluation report on the eligibility of the ESP-SAP recipients and the encoded SAC forms. Should the revalidation prove that the listed beneficiaries are indeed ineligible, we further recommended that the responsible City Officials refund the improperly provided subsidy to the 417 beneficiaries totaling ₱2,502,000.00, notwithstanding the inclusion of other ineligible recipients upon further validation pending submission of the aforestated reports from the CSWD and HRMO without prejudice to the demand for refund from the said beneficiaries as a matter of personal recourse.
observations.managementAction: The CSWDO revalidated and reconciled the list of beneficiaries and refunds are being processed where applicable.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the CSWDO revalidate the ineligibility of the 417 Job Order workers and that the HRMO review the remaining 1,089 names of other Job Order workers who may have been paid but were not included in the validated list. We further recommended that if the revalidation proves that the listed beneficiaries are indeed ineligible, the responsible City Officials refund the improperly provided subsidy to the 417 Job Order workers totaling ₱2,502,000.00, subject to any adjustments as a result of the CSWDO and HRMO validation of ineligible paid beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: The CSWDO is conducting revalidation and the HRMO will review the list. The CSWDO recommended refunds where applicable.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City demand for the refund of the total amount of ₱1,030,424.00 from the supplier CONEQUIP PHILIPPINES INC. to reimburse the amount paid by the City for the Notarial Fees and Documentary Stamps incurred in the opening of the Letters of Credit, the burden of which should be shouldered by the supplier as specified in Item 12 of the General Conditions of Contract and in accordance to the provisions of R.A. No. 9184. Otherwise, the payment shall be a personal liability of the officials who authorized the said irregular disbursement.
observations.managementAction: A request was sent to the supplier for refund. No return yet.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the CSWDO revalidate the ineligibility of the beneficiaries and submit the evaluation report on the eligibility of the ESP-SAP recipients and the encoded SAC forms. We likewise recommended that the City conduct a thorough revalidation of the listed ineligible beneficiaries and should the revalidation prove that the listed beneficiaries are indeed ineligible, that the responsible City Officials refund the improperly provided subsidy to the ineligible beneficiaries, without prejudice.
observations.managementAction: The CSWDO conducted revalidation and the results are being compiled for submission. Recommendations to refund have been issued where ineligible beneficiaries were found.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management secure the loan agreement together with BSP Monetary Board Opinion and BLGF-DOF certifications and also a certified true copy of SP RN 18-196 bearing the signatures of all concurring SP members on all pages, conduct a thorough assessment of the necessity of borrowing funds to avoid additional interest and costs, with primordial consideration of the City’s ability to repay the loan within the incumbency of the officials’ 3-year term in office, renegotiate on a fixed interest rate, adjust the SCBAA, and direct the Accountant to maintain a special account in the General Fund for loans secured.
observations.managementAction: None

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management give priority to this issue and seek the optimum alternative in the best interest of the public. We further recommended that an investigation on the circumstances and personalities involved be undertaken to assess the propriety of the said payment including a review of the existence of appropriations and available funds therefor and, if warranted, concrete legal action be taken against TWD for breach of contract with a demand for compensation for damages and interest over the length of time since payment was made considering the earnings that would have accrued to the City.
observations.managementAction: None

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require: 1) The Inventory Committee and the CGSO to prioritize the reconciliation of the RPCPPE with the accounting records, prepare the report for submission to the Auditor and pinpoint persons responsible for the missing properties who should be made to pay the value thereof as shown in the general ledger; 2) The CGSO to coordinate with the Accounting Office to fully account for the CY 2018 and 2019 PPE purchases, complete with data on the property tag, ARE, accountable officer, and office/location of the equipment, otherwise, those that cannot be accounted for shall be accordingly disallowed; and 3) The concerned department heads to prepare the Summaries of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit them to the Accounting Office, otherwise a determination should be made as to who should answer for the inventories which are no longer existent.
observations.managementAction: The GSO has been continuously reconciling its records and inventories to reconcile with the Accounting Office. However, no final report has been prepared. The GSO has submitted Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property to the Commission on Audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require: 1) The personnel concerned, particularly the signatories to the Individual Program of Work, the Abstract of Bids, canvass and Acceptance and Inspection Report, be made to answer for the alleged purchase of paints worth ₱250,000.00 and to refund such cost to the government; 2) Place upon the CHO the centralized accountability for all drugs and medical supplies by maintaining the stock cards for each item, requiring all departments, including barangays, to submit to the CHO their corresponding RIS with logbooks and proof of distribution of the items received with supporting documents, so that this could be consolidated with the CHO's own RIS to support the weekly or periodic SSMI to be submitted to the Accounting Office for proper recording; 3) Institutionalize guidelines in the release of medicines to avoid any ambiguity and promote fairness in availing such assistance from the government; and 4) Ensure that mitigating or compensating controls like periodic reconciliation of records and inventories, restricting release to qualified recipients and with complete documentation, updated recording and monitoring of consumption, are working effectively to reduce the risk of error or irregularities.
observations.managementAction: The CHO had submitted the Reports of Waste Materials together with the empty cartridges to the General Services Office for inspection by the auditor. The BAC to ensure compliance with ITB terms in procurement and request for CPR as indispensable requirement. Place upon the CHO centralized accountability for all drugs and medical supplies by maintaining stock cards; ensure periodic reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant and the City Treasurer send demand letters for the immediate liquidation of unliquidated cash advances/secure a copy of the duly-received transmittal letter for the liquidation of the intelligence funds, withhold payment of any money due to the accountable officer after due notice, and henceforth, strictly observe the requirements/limitations stipulated under Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-004; Exhaust all means for the settlement of the accountability of AOs who are already deceased or no longer in the service, and require the settlement of all money and property accountability of outgoing AOs prior to the release of the clearance, otherwise the joint liability clause under Section 104 of PD No. 1445 shall apply.
observations.managementAction: Demand Letters were sent to the concerned officials and employees. The CGSO is looking for remedies to comply with the requirement for insurance coverage.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management update and reconcile its records and inventories to reconcile with the Accounting Office. Investigate the whereabouts of equipment which were not found during inspection and submit a written explanation for the same; verify and coordinate and submit the complete listing of all Heavy Equipment and Agricultural and Forestry Equipment; and install logbooks and prescribed forms to monitor the use of each equipment.
observations.managementAction: The CGSO has been continuously reconciling its records and inventories to reconcile with the Accounting Office. They still have to investigate the equipment which were not found during inspection. CEO had assigned a Head at the Motorpool to monitor the status of all equipment under his jurisdiction and to safeguard equipment. The City Engineering Office had already submitted Certificates of Completion and Acceptance of the Project which were all attached to the Final Billings of the Contractor concerned. JEVs were prepared to record transfer of CIP to respective asset accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management secure the Transfer Certificate of Title to the lot purchased as an indispensable requirement, with a certified true copy of the SP resolution authorizing the purchase and the Zonal values in support of the valuation used, and an explanation why a downpayment exceeding 50% was made and the preparation of a PAC report and other documents.
observations.managementAction: The preent administration does not know the reason and action taken by the previous administration and the former General Services Officer. No Title in the name of the City Government of Tanjay for this particular lot exists in the Registry of Deeds.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the city government appropriate the necessary amount for premiums and insure with the General Insurance Fund assets amounting to ₱161.9 million against any insurable risk for compensation in the event of damages. Henceforth, the GSO must submit the budget proposal for every ensuing year.
observations.managementAction: The CGSO is still looking for remedies to comply with the requirement for insurance coverage.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCPC allocate more funds to PPAs responsive to the objective of providing protection to children against abuse, neglect, exploitation and violence and to prepare the required Development and Investment Plans for Children, and Local State of Children’s Report as required under DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120. We also required the LCPC to maintain an updated database of children, prepare the required quarterly meetings, maintain an updated database, prepare the required Development and Investment Plans for Children, and Local State of Children’s Report.
observations.managementAction: None

The CGSO must submit the budget proposal for every ensuing year.
observations.managementAction: The CGSO had started the process of acquiring insurance and is currently looking for remedies to comply with the requirement for insurance coverage.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the use of the Toyota Hi-Ace ambulance, backhoe excavator for ordinary, day-to-day operations be immediately stopped in order to preserve the utility of these vehicles to times of rescue and response activities in conformity with DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2014-155 dated December 17, 2014. Otherwise, if the LGU would wish to continue their present use, the LDRRMF should be reimbursed for the amount and the cost thereof.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO admitted the misuse and recommended reprogramming and reimbursement to the proper fund. LDRMO and City Budget Officer to coordinate for reimbursement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor reconstitute the committee created to investigate the theft of unserviceable property, which should be required to submit its report as soon as possible, and to immediately initiate the filing of appropriate charges against those found to have connived and participated therein.
observations.managementAction: None

observations.recommendation: We required the officials concerned to restitute the amounts used for the purchase of high-end cell phones and tablet computers totaling ₱198,593.00.
observations.managementAction: None

observations.managementAction: None

observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO recommended to the new Local Chief Executive for reimbursement. The LDRRMO admitted he did not know the status.

records and the necessary adjusting entries be prepared before the statement
date.

2.8. We recommended that the GSO prepare weekly the SSMI duly supported with
the RIS and other records and submit it to the Accounting Office as the basis
for recognizing the corresponding expense in the books. We likewise
recommended that the GSO and Accounting Office maintain stock cards and
ledger cards, respectively, for each type of inventory to facilitate reconciliation
with the actual stocks on hand.

3.12. We recommended that:
3.12.1. The General Services Office fully account for the fuel purchases based
on valid supporting documents, reconciling these with the actual balance
of fuel in the storage tank, to pinpoint responsibility for those that cannot
be accounted for, and to refrain from making additional purchases until
the balance is fully accounted for;
3.12.2. Management justify the necessity and reasonableness of the quantities of
fuel purchased, including the seven fuel procurements in December 2022,
and why these should not be considered excessive, given the huge
balance of inventory existing at year-end;
3.12.3. The GSO and Accounting Office invariably prepare and maintain the
records required under COA Circular No. 77-61, and to comply with all
the requirements therein, including the regular submission of the
consumption reports with complete supporting documents;
3.12.4. The personnel in charge of monitoring the gasoline storage tank submit
a written explanation of why fuel was released based on an unsigned
RIS;
3.12.5. The Internal Control Unit conduct spot checks to reconcile the remaining
fuel tank balance with the GSO records. It is likewise imperative that
the City establish a cut-off point to determine the level of fuel in the tank
and then accounts for current purchases while working on the
accountability for the prior purchases; and
3.12.6. Management ensure that mitigating or compensating controls like
periodic reconciliation of records and inventories, restricting fuel
release based on a duly approved RIS, and updated recording and
consumption monitoring are working effectively to reduce the risk of
error or irregularities.

4.10. We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Agriculturist and the
City Veterinarian to prepare the Updated Schedule of Biological Assets of the
City at the end of each reporting period and submit the same to the City
Accounting Office for the immediate recognition of adjustments in the City’s
books of accounts. The schedule should reflect the fair value less costs to sell
for each biological asset (especially for breeding stocks), any additions resulting
from birth, and reductions due to deaths and distributions.

4.11. We further recommended that the City Inventory Committee conduct a
physical count of all the biological assets recorded in the books of accounts of
Page 21 of 82
the City and that the GSO and the Accountant maintain the property cards and
WOABSLC, respectively, and reconcile their records.

5.9. We recommended that Management strictly comply with the requirements of
Sections 85 and 86 of P.D. No. 1445 when hiring JOs, and to provide justification
why they should not be held personally liable for the wages paid to them that
were not backed by sufficient appropriations and had no existing contracts to
render service.

6.7. We recommended that the HRMO coordinate with the Local Finance
Committee to ensure the existence of appropriations and completion of
approved JO appointments/contracts before the JO workers render their
services to the City, and that the City Mayor require the disbursing officers and
all other concerned city officials to process the cash advances for the payment
of wages to JO workers within five days after the end of the pay period.

7.11. We recommended that:
7.11.1. In accordance with Item 13.1 of CSC-DBM JC No. 1 series of 2015, the
City Mayor, through the City HRM Officer, formulate and adopt
internal rules and procedures on the rendition of overtime services,
which shall be aligned with relevant rules and regulations such as, but
not limited to, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and CSC-DBM JC No. 1
series of 2015. When formulating these rules and procedures,
Management should consider that, generally, overtime services should be
compensated through CTO, following the guidelines under the CSC-
DBM JCs No. 2, s. 2004 with some amendments under CSC-DBM JC No.
2-1 series of 2005 dated July 1, 2005. However, cash payment for
overtime services through Overtime Pay may be authorized only in
exceptional cases when the application of CTO for all overtime hours
would negatively impact the agency’s operations;
7.11.2. The City Mayor desist from issuing blanket authorities but rather ensure
that the Request for Authority is specific to the purpose or expected
outputs and includes the specific period of the overtime services
(indicating the specific date, time, and number of hours). Most
importantly, Management should warrant that only necessary requests
for overtime services are approved, considering the responsible use of
government funds, and that requests are approved before actual
rendition of OT, with proper monitoring to be done by the HRMO;
7.11.3. The City Accountant ensure compliance with Item 10.1 of CSC-DBM JC
No. 1 series of 2015. As such, only employees who arrive on or before
Page 26 of 82
the start of the workday shall be allowed to render overtime work with
pay, provided that at least 2 hours of overtime services are rendered.
Also, the City Accountant ensure that claims are supported with
complete documentary requirements for overtime pay;
7.11.4. Prospectively, in compliance with Item 12.0 of CSC-DBM JC No. 1 series
of 2015, the City Mayor, through the HRMO, ensure that the LGU
submits to the DBM's Budget and Management Bureau or Regional
Office concerned, copy furnished the CSC, the "Report on Overtime
Services With Pay" using the template in Annex A of the JC on or before
March 31 of every year; and
7.11.5. Submit a written explanation for the noted deficiencies.

7.12. We further recommended that deficient claims for overtime pay be refunded in
view of the City’s non-compliance with the regulations.

8.8. We recommended that Management desist from granting cash advances to the
Disbursing Officers in amounts exceeding their maximum cash accountability
pursuant to the provisions outlined in Treasury Circular No. 02-2019 and
likewise reduce the amounts granted as determined to be necessary within the
allowed periods provided under COA Circular No. 97-002.

8.9. We also recommended that Management evaluate the sufficiency of the
existing bond coverage of the Disbursing Officers vis-a-vis their actual
accountabilities in accordance with the revised schedule provided in the TC.

9.10. We recommended that the City Treasurer, in coordination with the SP and the
City Legal Officer: (i) develop an effective tax collection campaign program to
enhance the collection of RPT/SET Receivables; (ii) submit the annual certified
list of RPT delinquencies on or before December 31 of each year, stating the
reasons for non-collection and requesting assistance from the SP in the
enforcement of the remedies; and (iii) enforce remedies in the collection of real
property tax through the issuance of a warrant on or before, or simultaneously
with, the institution of the civil action for the collection of the delinquent tax.

10.12. We recommended that the City Assessor prepare updated SMVs reflective of the
current market pursuant to Section 212 of the LGC and cause the drafting of an
Ordinance for consideration of the Sanggunian, incorporating therein the
proposed SMV, assessment level, and tax rate. We further recommended that
the Sanggunian give priority and due course to this undertaking.

10.13. Henceforth, we recommended that the City strictly adhere to the provisions of
Section 219 of the LGC and DILG-DOF JMC No. 2010-01 in the preparation and
completion of the general revision of real property assessments within the
reglementary period of three years.

11.10. We recommended that the City Accountant reconcile the reciprocal accounts by
reviewing the transactions affecting the Due to/from Other Funds accounts,
effect adjustments to reflect the correct account balances, and present reliable
data on the City’s financial position as of a given date of time.

12.11. We recommended that Management submit proof that their request to avail of
the e-NGAS has been denied and secure the corresponding authority to avail of
other accounting softwares, otherwise, continued availment of a non-e-NGAS
accounting software shall be deemed irregular. Should this be accomplished,
we recommended that before continuing to use the ECPAC accounting software
any further:
12.11.1. Management require ECPAC to upgrade its system to include features
similar to e-NGAS at no additional cost and specify the expected
outputs in the contract; and
12.11.2. The City Accountant require ECPAC to correct the deficiencies noted in
the initial use of the system and defer any further disbursements until the
City is completely satisfied with the delivery of the expected outputs.
Otherwise, hold responsible the officials who effected the procurement of an
unprescribed system.

13.11. We recommended that, henceforth, Management closely monitor invariable
compliance with the submission of monthly and quarterly financial reports
with complete schedules and monthly collection and disbursement accounts in
accordance with the requirements of pertinent laws and regulations.

observations.managementAction: Per letter dated 10/15/2021, informal 2014 meetings with some members of Tanjay City Water District Board of Directors (TWD-BOD) were made. The City Accountant and City Treasurer in a letter dated July 5, 2020 sought the assistance of the City Legal Officer, who, in turn, wrote the BOD of TWD on August 10, 2020 seeking payment/refund on the advances made in liquidation of TWD’s loan obligation with LWUA by the City in the amount of ₱30,161,004.95, apart from the interest thereon. The City Legal Office is of the view that a direct legal action against TWD will only bring disaster on LGU’s claim for reimbursement of the Php30M paid to LWUA in liquidation of TWD’s loan; Thus the better option is to take the diplomatic route rather than judicial confrontation, which is costly, time-consuming if not frustrating and may not bring to fore the desired result.
observations.reason: Given the length of time, approximately 19 years, since the City paid TWD’s loan without any action on the latter’s part, and despite the leniency with which this transaction was afforded, this gives no recourse but for the Team to issue a corresponding disallowance for this payment. Management opted for diplomatic rather than judicial action. No settlement or refund was received.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to
compute the amortization of its Intangible Assets account for calendar year 2021
and backwards. Prior years’ amortization should be taken up as Prior Period
Adjustment in the books to be closed to Government Equity at the end of the
reporting period.

We recommended that the City Accountant review the transactions
affecting the Due to/from Other Funds accounts, including that of prior years, and
effect adjustments to reconcile the reciprocal account balances and present
reliable data on the City’s financial position as of a given period of time.

We recommended that Management require the DSWD as the
requisitioner of the food supplies purchased, in coordination with the DRRMO,
to submit the daily menu for meals and the persons to whom these were served to
the Audit Team for validation, encapsulated in SSMIs supported with the RIS
which shall serve as basis for the Accountant in recognizing the expenses in the
books. Likewise, we recommended that the end-user and the BAC adhere strictly
to Section 7.3.2 (f) of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, including the submission
of Official Receipts/Sales Invoice to complement the Collection Receipts issued.
Moreover, we recommended that Management ensure that mitigating or
compensating controls like periodic reconciliation of records and inventories,
restricting release to qualified recipients and with complete documentation,
updated recording and monitoring of consumption, are working effectively to
reduce the risk of error or irregularities.

We recommended that Management submit the DOT Certificates of
Authority to Operate of the hotels engaged by the City as quarantine facilities,
provide the certified list of persons with complete information who were
accommodated in these hotels and justify the need to incur additional costs for
housing these persons in private hotels instead of government facilities.

We recommended that the City Treasurer strictly observe the regulations
governing receipt of government funds, seeing to that collections are remitted and
deposited intact and on a daily basis or on the next banking day, whichever comes
first, and to closely monitor personnel who may be delegated with such functions.

We recommended that the City Accountant conduct a thorough analysis of the entries made in 2020, as well as that of CYs 2018 and 2019 where surplus was also reported, and to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correctly reflect the trust receipts already expended for capital outlays so that the balances of the Other Deferred Credits, income and government equity accounts may be fairly presented in the financial statements. We further recommended that Management determine whether the purpose for the trust receipt was already fulfilled or the project completed so that the corresponding revenue may be taken out from the Other Deferred Credits account. Henceforth, ensure strict compliance to the PPSAS and the GAM for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: Thorough review and analysis were made on the entries as recommended and the corresponding Adjusting entries were made per GJ 300-21-06-007(1) dated 6/30/2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant and the City Treasurer prioritize the review of these recorded payables to ascertain if these still represent valid claims to the extent possible, otherwise seek authority from the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the City Mayor to request for write-off of the account balances from the COA duly supported by the requirements stipulated under Section III.A.10 of COA Circular No. 97-001 so that these may be made available for funding valuable development programs and other priority activities of the City.
observations.managementAction: Accounts Payable and Other Payables are being reviewed. As of August 31, 2021 – As per review of the Accounts Payable and Other Payables accounts, it was found out that included therein are: Payables to suppliers that cannot be reverted to the unappropriated surplus since payments of these claims are pending due to incomplete bidding documents of the supplier. ₱1,000,000.00 transfer received from the National Government that the city ought to return since the purpose of which is unknown but the DBM does not allow the return of the fund since they themselves cannot determine the purpose of the transfer (Update as of December 31, 2021)

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the City Treasurer to invariably prepare the Schedule of Unreleased Checks on the last working day of the year and to submit this to the City Accountant as basis in preparing the journal entry to restore the cash in bank balance and to recognize the related liability accounts at the end of the year, and likewise as basis for the reversing entry to be made on the first day of the ensuing year. A copy of the request letter for the Schedule of Unreleased Checks dated 12/29/2021 signed by the City Accountant and the Statement of Unreleased Checks as of December 31, 2021 signed by the City Treasurer are hereto attached along with the copies of the JEV Nos. CK 100-21-12-0086 dated 12/31/2021 and CK 100-22-01-0001 dated 1/3/2022. Recommendation is well taken. The CTO will prepare the schedule of unreleased check at the end of the year.
observations.managementAction: The necessary Journal Entry Voucher was made to restore the Cash in Bank balance and to recognize the related liability accounts at the end of the year. Likewise, the reversing entry was also made on the first day of the ensuing year. A copy of the request letter for the Schedule of Unreleased Checks dated 12/29/2021 signed by the City Accountant and the Statement of Unreleased Checks as of December 31, 2021 signed by the City Treasurer are hereto attached along with the copies of the JEV Nos. CK 100-21-12-0086 dated 12/31/2021 and CK 100-22-01-0001 dated 1/3/2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit a written explanation why payments from the LCs were issued to Big Yellow instead of CONEQUIP and why payments were made against the LCs despite the lack of delivery of goods. We further recommended that the electronic fund transfers for the first five releases of the LCs, the corresponding promissory notes, and the sales invoice be submitted to the Office of the Auditor for further verification.
observations.managementAction: Copies of the Promissory Notes were already submitted through a Transmittal Letter dated June 7, 2021. Electronic Fund Transfers and Sales Invoices. A request letter dated June 17, 2021 was already sent to the Land Bank of the Philippines – Negros Oriental Lending Center for the documents requested. These letters were already submitted through a Transmittal Letter dated 8/5/2021 which is hereto attached as Annex A.2

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management comply with DBM-DOH JC No. 1, s. 2012 as amended, Supreme Court Decision under G.R. 207145, DBM-DSWD JC No. 1, s. 2018 and establish a procedure for identifying PHWs and PSWs who shall be exposed to high risk or low risk hazard including the duration of such exposure duly approved by the Secretary of Health to support claims for regular hazard pay. We further recommended that the City Accountant require all PHWs and PSWs to support their claims for regular hazard pay with proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks with certifications from appropriate agencies, including the documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 as an indispensable requirement. All payments not compliant with the regulations cannot be allowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: All PHWs and PSWs are already required to support their claims for regular hazard pay with proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks including certifications from appropriate agencies, including the documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 starting June 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City demand for the refund of the total amount of ₱1,030,424.00 from CONEQUIP PHILIPPINES INC. to reimburse the amount paid by the City for the Notarial Fees and Documentary Stamps incurred in the opening of the Letters of Credit, the burden of which should be shouldered by the supplier as specified in Item 12 of the General Conditions of Contract and in accordance to the provisions of R.A. No. 9184. Otherwise, the payment shall be a personal liability of the officials who authorized said irregular disbursement. We further recommended that the City Accounting Office make the necessary journal entry to recognize a receivable account from CONEQUIP PHILIPPINES INC. and adjust the Construction and Heavy Equipment account in the books.
observations.managementAction: A request letter for reimbursement dated June 23, 2021 was already sent to the Board of Directors of CONEQUIP PHILIPPINES INC. Furthermore, the necessary adjusting entries were already made per JEV 100-21-06-0031 dated 6/25/2021.
observations.reason: No proof of refund by Conequip Philippines, Inc. was submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant prepare a correcting entry to transfer the said collections totaling ₱366,595.00 from the Trust Fund books to the General Fund books and to likewise observe henceforth the same principle in recording all receipts of similar nature.
observations.managementAction: The said collections were already transferred through Disbursement Voucher No. CK 300-21-06-032 dated 6/24/2021 and OR No. 6946944 dated 7/2/2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City, through the CSWDO, submit the evaluation report on the eligibility of the ESP-SAP recipients and the encoded SAC forms. We likewise recommended that the City conduct a thorough revalidation of the ineligible beneficiaries. Should the revalidation prove that the listed beneficiaries are indeed ineligible, we further recommended that the responsible City Officials refund the improperly provided subsidy to the 35 beneficiaries totaling to ₱210,000.00, notwithstanding the inclusion of other ineligible recipients upon further validation pending submission of the aforestated reports from the CSWD and without prejudice to the demand for refund from the said beneficiaries as a matter of personal recourse.
observations.managementAction: The CSWDO in her reply which we received on March 9, 2022 stated that it is the LGU which assesses and determines the eligible recipients of the SAP. She stated that their office evaluated the SAC forms and submitted the SAC forms to the audit team. She further stated that her office revalidated the eligibility of the 35 beneficiaries, the results of which were attached as Annex 2 to her letter-reply. The hard copies of the SAC forms were submitted on August 13, 2021, not the AOM which was dated May 20, 2021, almost three months after. Request for the said documents were made even earlier, prior to the AOM issuance.
observations.reason: The hard copies of the SAC forms were submitted on August 13, 2021, not the AOM which was dated May 20, 2021, almost three months after. Request for the said documents were made even earlier, prior to the AOM issuance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management justify the necessity of procuring multiple heavy equipment given the above observations, submit the loan agreement together with the BSP Monetary Board Opinion and BLGF-DOF certifications and also a certified true copy of SP RN 18-196 bearing the signatures of all concurring SP members on all pages, conduct a thorough assessment of the necessity of borrowing funds to avoid additional interest and costs, with primordial consideration of the City’s ability to repay the loan within the incumbency of the officials’ 3-year term in office, renegotiate on a fixed interest rate, adjust the SCBAA, and direct the Accountant to maintain a special account in the General Fund for loans secured.
observations.managementAction: The loans are monitored through a Subsidiary Ledger with account no. 2-01-02-040 under the account title: Loans Payable – Domestic. Management submitted a copy of the loan agreement. The issued Notice of Suspension No. 2020-01-100-(19) which is separately issued, hence, evaluation of these documents shall be taken as a whole.
observations.reason: Management submitted a copy of the loan agreement. No separate assessment on the necessity of borrowing funds, no renegotiation on the fixed interest rate, no adjustments were made in the SCBAA, and no special account was maintained.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) Exercise the remedies provided under Section 8.4, Annex “E” of the RIRR in addressing the contracts under default, without prejudice to other courses of action and remedies available under the circumstances; b) Impose and collect liquidated damages on erring contractors in accordance with Section 8.5, Annex “E” of the RIRR for projects that have incurred negative slippage of more than 10%; c) Require the City Engineer, City Planning and Development Officer (CPDO) and other officials to submit a written explanation why no immediate steps were undertaken to address the deficiencies noted, for the seeming lack of initiative to enforce the measures available to the government to correct such defects, why progress payments were allowed for projects with negative slippage, and why final payment was approved for projects not compliant with specifications; d) Justify the obvious tendency of implementing projects by contract given the additional indirect cost incurred and why the same should not be regarded as unnecessary expenses which are not allowed under COA Circular No. 2012-003; and e) Implement measures and establish coordination mechanisms to ensure that projects are closely monitored so that any delays may be promptly corrected and other courses of action/remedies may be availed of.
observations.managementAction: Liquidated damages were imposed as per last billing of the project. Delayed implementation was due to bad weather and sometimes road was unpassable leading to the project and RROW. The Former OIC-CPDC in her statement, indicated incapability to address and make an update on the project due to health conditions; She has undergone chemotherapy and was on work-from-home arrangement. The CEO requested the contractors concerned to fast track the implementation and completion. The City Engineer reported that projects were completed, however, implementation was delayed due to bad weather and RROW. The quarterly reports were submitted but the PPAs still does not contain all the necessary information, indicative of the deficient monitoring by the concerned offices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, thru the City Development Council, conduct an in-depth assessment of the priority needs of the City so as to eliminate the need for project realignments. We also recommended that the City Engineer improve the execution and monitoring of projects being implemented in order to timely address backlogs and problems which may be encountered so as not to hamper completion thereof. We further recommended that the City Engineer maintain an updated list of projects with correct and complete details to facilitate proper monitoring.
observations.managementAction: More projects were implemented and completed but liquidation reports were delayed. The CEO requested the contractors concerned to fast track the implementation and completion. The quarterly reports were submitted. The CEO has been remiss in supporting the quarterly report with any project suspensions, extensions, resumptions duly approved by the City and acknowledged by the contractor inspite of a standing instruction to do so, hence lending no credibility to the project delays noted.
observations.reason: Backlogs were still substantial in number.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Engineer assign more personnel to assist in the preparation of the related duties to accelerate the implementation of projects funded out of the 20% DF and to fast track the backlogs in prior years in order to minimize idle funds and maximize the usefulness of such projects. We further recommended that the City Engineer enhance monitoring of project implementation by maintaining an updated list of projects and taking corrective measures for projects that are lagging far behind schedule. Any favorable action given hereto will ultimately redound to the benefit of the city and its constituents, and to the achievement of the overall objective of the government to make LGUs self-reliant communities.
observations.managementAction: Quarterly reports as to the physical status of the project were submitted. Delayed implementation due to RROW problem and unpassable road.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor stop the deployment of job order employees to barangays and national government agencies and to consider limiting or reducing the number of job order workers to be hired to what is necessary for the implementation of projects such that the wages of these laborers will be correctly charged to the projects being implemented. Plans to create new plantilla position is put on hold since the City has reached the PS cap.
observations.managementAction: Stopped the deployment of JOs to barangays and national government agencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accountant assign additional personnel for the sorting and preparation of vouchers to be submitted to the auditor for post-audit and to monitor and ensure the timely preparation and submission of the quarterly trial balances and transaction documents within the reglementary periods.
observations.managementAction: According to the City Accounting Office, a scanner was set-up starting November 2021 to aid the filing of the documentary requirements to eliminate segregation process and duplicate copies. Segregation of original and duplicate copies is not an acceptable reason for the delays which can reach months and even almost a year, especially those by contract.

observations.recommendation: Required the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to bill the COMELEC for the amount disbursed by the city as honoraria to the Board of Inspectors in the May 14, 2007 National and Local Elections, otherwise, require the recipients to refund the amount collected. We further recommended that the bill be sent to the local COMELEC inasmuch as the honoraria was not coursed thru the national COMELEC but paid directly to the BEIs.
observations.managementAction: Billing Statement was already sent to the COMELEC previously. The City Treasurer had difficulty locating the BEIs given the length of time that has passed.
observations.reason: No actual restitution made by COMELEC nor the BEIs themselves.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: a) Replenish the 20% DF from the General Fund Proper, through an appropriation ordinance, the cost of administrative projects, e.g. landscaping and park improvements, installation of elevator and the purchase of a motorcycle and henceforth desist from appropriating the 20% DF for projects that are not compliant with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1; b) Realign the lump-sum appropriations for non-specific projects and anti-illegal drugs campaign totaling ₱29,497,455.56 to specific development projects with line by line allocation of inputs in a pre-determined location clearly indicated; c) Submit thru the CDC and the LFC their evaluation of the projects whose appropriations were reverted, with a categorical assessment as to its necessity in order to guide us in the evaluation of similar projects which were equally appropriated, as well as an explanation why the new items budgeted from the reversions were not included in the local development plan for inclusion in the annual investment plan, not just an addendum, given the extent of its financial requirements; d) Closely monitor thru the City Engineering Office the completion of the Rizal Park projects in accordance with specifications and within the timelines stipulated in the contracts, completed, notwithstanding the results of the technical evaluation of the contracts.
observations.managementAction: The Budget Office stated that the CDC did not invite the LFC during its planning session from CY 2019 up to the last budget year of 2021. Project extensions, suspensions, resumptions were not attached to the quarterly PPAs as instructed. The City Engineer said the project was completed but implementation was delayed due to bad weather conditions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: a) Thru the GSO update its records to reconcile with the accounting records and investigate the whereabouts of the equipment totaling ₱3,844,968.00 which were not found during the inspection, submitting a written explanation for the same; b) Thru the CGSO and the City Engineer verify and submit documents for the heavy equipment for costing ₱750,000.00 and agricultural and forest equipment costing ₱2,597,000.00 which were actually inspected but not found in the books; c) Thru the City Engineer maintain a separate logbook for each equipment to monitor its use and ensure that the use thereof is for official purposes duly authorized by the approving officials using a prescribed form, like the Request for Issuance of Service Vehicle used by the Department of Public Works and Highways, where the project name and duration of use is clearly indicated, with supporting Driver’s Trip Ticket and the Monthly Report of Travels; and d) Thru the City Engineer install physical safeguards and controls to protect equipment from loss, pilferage or unnecessary exposure to damage or improper use, including the repair of those that are out of order, so that maximum use thereof can be availed by the government.
observations.managementAction: All heavy equipment were monitored as to the status of operation in the Motorpool. One bulldozer is operational but was left at Brgy. Sto. Nino and is not anymore operational. The CGSO stated that a single logbook monitoring the movement of all heavy equipment is used.
observations.reason: Management did not provide any comment on the action taken to implement the recommendations. The CGSO has not provided any documents to the Accounting Office as basis for reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the City Treasurer to prioritize the preparation of the certified list of taxpayers and the taxes due by assigning personnel to update the RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s index cards, a summary of which must be submitted to the City Accountant as basis for setting up the SET receivables at the beginning of the year in accordance with PPSAS and Section 20 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I, informing the team that a soft copy of the RPTAR covering CYs 2016 to 2018 will be furnished to the accounting office immediately in order to update the previously recorded tax receivables.
observations.managementAction: Corrective measures were already done and a soft copy was submitted to the accounting office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: a) Require the OIC-CGSO to submit a written explanation why the equipment was not included in the inventory list for inspection and, with the City Accountant, why there is a constant delay in the submission of bidding documents to support the disbursement vouchers; b) Require the supplier to submit the required warranty/security and other bid documents; and c) Take appropriate administrative/legal action where warranted.
observations.managementAction: CGSO, in its letter dated October 21, 2018, admitted that it failed to follow the regular procurement procedures for this manlift. Payment was made thru a Letter of Credit even without delivery yet in CY 2017. The manlift was delivered in CY 2018 and included in the inventory in CY 2019. All documents submitted will be taken into consideration upon final evaluation in audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: a) consider terminating the contract, forfeiting the performance security, deducting liquidated damages from whatever payment will be due to the supplier, and imposing the sanctions for blacklisting per GPPB guidelines; b) direct the GSO to accept all deliveries before having them inspected by the requesting units and to monitor completeness of deliveries with corresponding measures to address erring suppliers; and c) designate an inspector for each health unit who will be responsible for checking the correctness of the medicines and supplies delivered to them.
observations.managementAction: GSO accepts all deliveries, subject to inspection together with the end-user. The CHO did not provide any comment on this recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit the required documents enumerated above, together with an explanation why it made a downpayment of more than 50% of the contracted amount which is contrary to Section 6.10 of RA No. 10752, and to secure the Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) to the lot purchased as an indispensable requirement. We further recommended that the City Appraisal Committee submit documents to support its observance of the standards laid out in Section 12 of RA No. 10752 in determining the market value of the lots purchased.
observations.managementAction: The City Appraisal Committee furnished copies of documents submitted in determining the market value of the lot. The City Assessor informed the Team in her letter dated October 12, 2021 that contrary to the recommendation, it is not her office to process the said document nor does her office hold the original and duplicate copies of titles on properties owned by the City Government.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer, thru the Market Collectors Office, intensify collection of rentals due from the different delinquent stall owners by sending demand letters and by applying the remedies that are provided for under Sections 13 and 15.10 of the City of Tanjay Market Code of 2005 as amended. We further recommended that delinquent payments covered by compromise agreements be regularly updated to ensure that the terms set forth therein are diligently followed by the delinquent stall owner, otherwise demand full and immediate collection of the total delinquency.
observations.managementAction: Sent demand letters to delinquent stall owners. Entered into compromise agreements and reshuffled collectors. Ongoing collections reduced delinquent rentals to ₱325,316.76.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the city government appropriate the necessary amount for premiums and insure with the General Insurance Fund assets amounting to ₱161.9 million against any insurable risk such as fire, earthquake or other calamities in order that it can be compensated for any damages or losses thereof.
observations.managementAction: Funds for the payment of insurance premiums are now available.
observations.reason: No proof submitted as to the actual payment of insurance for these properties.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Engineer prepare and submit to the City Accountant copies of the Certificates of Completion and Acceptance of the projects which have already been completed but are still carried in the CIP account, and for the latter to take up the appropriate journal entry reclassifying the cost of the projects from CIP to the appropriate asset account and to recognize depreciation on these assets in order to fairly present the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Accounting Office is currently reviewing records. It further requested that details of the ₱300 million as to the particular references in billings were very slow. Records can be found in Annex 15 of the CY 2016 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that in the preparation of its Annual Work and Financial Plan, the LCPC allocate more funds to PPAs responsive to the objective of providing protection to children against abuse, neglect, exploitation and violence and promotion of their rights and welfare as envisioned under RA No. 9344. Moreover, we recommended that it conduct quarterly meetings, maintain an updated database of children, prepare the required Development and Investment Plans for Children, and Local State of Children’s Report as required under DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120. Lastly, we recommended that the cost of the already allocated service vehicle be charged to the General Fund and reimbursed to the LCPC fund.

observations.recommendation: Required the City Treasurer’s Office to lay down the requirements for the data and reports that need to be generated by their office then require the supplier to install the programs for generating the specified computerized reports. observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that Management require: a) The Inventory Committee and the CGSO to prioritize the reconciliation of the RPCPPE with the accounting records, prepare the report for submission to the Auditor and pinpoint persons responsible for the missing properties, who should be made to pay the value thereof as shown in the general ledger; the balances in the RPCPPE and in the accounting books cannot be reconciled. b) The CGSO to coordinate with the Accounting Office to fully account for the CY 2018 and 2019 PPE purchases, complete with data on the property tag, ARE, accountable officer, and office/location of the equipment, otherwise, those that cannot be accounted for shall be accordingly disallowed; and c) The concerned department heads to prepare the Summaries of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit them to the Accounting Office, otherwise a determination should be made as to who should answer for the inventories which are no longer existent.
