observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant conduct a thorough analysis
of the entries made in which income was recognized in the Trust Fund books for
CY 2020, as well as that of CYs 2018 and 2019 where surplus was also reported,
and to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correctly reflect the trust
receipts already expended for capital outlays so that the balances of the Other
Deferred Credits, income and government equity accounts may be fairly
presented in the financial statements. We further recommended that
Management determine whether the purpose for the trust receipt was already
fulfilled or the project completed so that the corresponding revenue may be
taken out from the Other Deferred Credits account. Henceforth, ensure strict
compliance to the PPSAS and the GAM for LGUs.

We recommended that the City Accountant and the City Treasurer
prioritize the review of these recorded payables to ascertain if these still
represent valid claims to the extent possible, otherwise seek authority from the
Sangguniang Panlungsod for the City Mayor to request for write-off of the
account balances from the COA duly supported by the requirements stipulated
under Section III.A.10 of COA Circular No. 97-001 so that these may be made
available for funding valuable development programs and other priority
activities of the City and so that the liabilities would be fairly presented in the
financial statements. We further recommended that the City Accountant assign
additional personnel to work on the preparation of the aging schedules and
other important details of the four liability accounts earlier discussed so that
these would be properly kept and presented in the financial statement to aid
users thereof in decision-making.

We recommended that Management require the City Treasurer to
invariably prepare the Schedule of Unreleased Checks on the last working day of
the year and to submit this to the City Accountant as basis in preparing the
journal entry to restore the cash in bank balance and to recognize the related
liability accounts at the end of the year, and likewise as basis for the reversing
entry to be made on the first day of the ensuing year.

We recommended that Management require CONEQUIP PHILIPPINES
INC. to submit the warranty security in the required form as per Section 62 of
R.A. No. 9184 on the awarded contract of the Procurement of Brand New Heavy
Equipment and Vehicles totaling ₱137,389,164.00, and subsequently submit the
same to the Office of the Auditor for further verification.

We further recommended the submission of the lacking documents as
prescribed under DILG Memorandum Circular (MC) No. 2014-155 as amended,
as well as the other documentary requirements discussed above.

Lastly, we recommended that Management submit justification as
regards the provision of Administrative Order No. 227 and the categorical
advantage for importing heavy equipment instead of sourcing it from domestic
suppliers.

We recommended that Management submit a written explanation why
payments from the LCs were issued to Big Yellow instead of CONEQUIP and
why payments were made against the LCs despite the lack of delivery of goods.
We further recommeneded that the electronic fund transfers for the first five
releases of the LCs, the corresponding promissory notes, and the sales invoice be
submitted to the Office of the Auditor for further verification.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) comply
with the mandatory posting of the updated APP, NOA, and other information
relative to the Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act in the GPPB
Online Portal as required in GPPB Circular No. 01-2020. Henceforth, we
recommended that the BAC should always be updated with the issuances and
advisories of the GPPB by visiting their website regularly to avoid any more
non-compliance in the future.

We recommended that Management substantiate the propriety of the
procurement of the food items totaling ₱55,362,150.00 charged to the BGCM
distributed to the households, providing written explanations/justifications and
documentary evidence to each of the issues discussed above, for further
evaluation.

We recommended that Management, through the City Social Welfare
and Development Officer, submit a written explanation with documentary
evidence on the existence of the following deficiencies:
a. Differences between the total quantities of the goods distributed as reflected
in the submitted Summary of BGCM Relief Goods Distribution to 24
Barangays by the CSWDO and the Relief Goods Distribution Sheets
prepared by Barangay Health Workers on the 14 barangays;
b. Differences between the reported quantity of relief goods received by the
beneficiaries/recipients and the actual quantity received by the beneficiaries
as validated through the duly complied COA confirmation letters by the
sample population of beneficiaries, including the other individual deficiencies
noted above;
c. Duplication of names and signatures that do not match.
We further recommended that Management provide sufficient
justification on the deficiencies noted.

We recommended that the CSWDO revalidate the ineligibility of the 417
Job Order workers and that the HRMO review the remaining 1,089 names of
other Job Order workers who may have been paid but were not included in the
validated list. We further recommended that the responsible City Officials
refund the ESP given to the 417 Job Order workers totaling ₱2,502,000.00,
subject to any adjustments as a result of the CSWDO and HRMO validation of
ineligible paid beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management comply with DBM-DOH JC No. 1, s.
2012 as amended, Supreme Court Decision under G.R. 207145, DBM-DSWD JC
No. 1, s. 2018 and establish a procedure for identifying PHWs and PSWs who
shall be exposed to high risk or low risk hazard including the duration of such
exposure duly approved by the Secretary of Health to support claims for regular
hazard pay. We further recommended that the City Accountant require all
PHWs and PSWs to support their claims for regular hazard pay with proof of
actual exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks with
certifications from appropriate agencies, including the documents required
under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 as an indispensable
requirement. All payments not compliant with the regulations cannot be
allowed in audit.

We recommended that the City demand for the refund of the total
amount of ₱1,030,424.00 from the supplier CONEQUIP PHILIPPINES INC. to
reimburse the amount paid by the City for the Notarial Fees and Documentary
Stamps incurred in the opening of the Letters of Credit, the burden of which
should be shouldered by the supplier as specified in Item 12 of the General
Conditions of Contract and in accordance to the provisions of R.A. No. 9184.
Otherwise, the payment shall be a personal liability of the officials who
authorized the said irregular disbursement.

We further recommended that the City Accounting Office make the
necessary journal entry to recognize a receivable account from CONEQUIP
PHILIPPINES INC. and adjust the Construction and Heavy Equipment account
in the books.

We recommended that the City Accountant prepare a correcting entry to
transfer the said collections totaling ₱366,595.00 from the Trust Fund books to
the General Fund books and to likewise observe henceforth the same principle in
recording all receipts of similar nature.

We recommended that the City, through the CSWDO, submit the
evaluation report on the eligibility of the ESP-SAP recipients and the encoded
SAC forms. We likewise recommended that the City conduct a thorough
revalidation of the listed ineligible beneficiaries. Should the revalidation prove
that the listed beneficiaries are indeed ineligible, we further recommended that
the responsible City Officials refund the improperly provided subsidy to the 35
beneficiaries totaling ₱210,000.00, notwithstanding the inclusion of other
ineligible recipients upon further validation pending submission of the
aforestated reports from the CSWD and without prejudice to the demand for
refund from the said beneficiaries as a matter of personal recourse.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) Exercise the remedies provided under Section 8.4, Annex “E” of the RIRR in addressing the contracts under default, without prejudice to other courses of action and remedies available under the circumstances; b) Impose and collect liquidated damages on erring contractors in accordance with Section 8.5, Annex “E” of the RIRR for projects that have incurred negative slippage of more than 10%; c) Require the City Engineer, City Planning and Development Officer (CPDO) and other officials to submit a written explanation why no immediate steps were undertaken to address the deficiencies noted, for the seeming lack of initiative to enforce the measures available to the government to correct such defects, why progress payments were allowed for projects with negative slippage, and why final payment was approved for projects not compliant with specifications; d) Justify the obvious tendency of implementing projects by contract given the additional indirect cost incurred and why the same should not be regarded as unnecessary expenses which are not allowed under COA Circular No. 2012-003; and e) Implement measures and establish coordination mechanisms to ensure that projects are closely monitored so that any delays may be promptly corrected and other courses of action/remedies may be availed of.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to: a) Require the CHO to fully account for the medicines and medical supplies purchased in CY 2018 so we may validate these with the records obtained during the ocular inspection conducted so we may validate these with the records obtained during the ocular inspection conducted as basis for adjusting the corresponding inventory and expense accounts reported in the financial statements; and b) Direct the BAC be fully aware of the terms in the ITB in order to act accordingly in the evaluation of bids and strictly require bidders to invariably indicate the brand names and to submit the CPR as an indispensable requirement to provide assurance that the government receives an equitable value for the expenditures incurred and explain the incurrence of these deficiencies in the subject procurement; c) Place upon the CHO the centralized accountability for all drugs and medical supplies by maintaining the stock cards for each item, requiring all departments, including barangays, to submit to the CHO their corresponding RIS with logbooks and proof of distribution of the items received with supporting documents, so that this could be consolidated with the CHO’s own RIS to support the weekly or periodic SSMI to be submitted to the Accounting Office for proper recording; d) Institutionalize guidelines in the release of medicines to avoid any ambiguity and promote fairness in the availment of such assistance from the government; and e) Ensure that mitigating or compensating controls like periodic reconciliation of records and inventories, restricting release to qualified recipients and with complete documentation, updated recording and monitoring of consumption, are working effectively to reduce the risk of error or irregularities.

We likewise recommended that the BAC Secretariat and City Accountant submit a written explanation for the constant delay in the submission of bidding documents to support the disbursement vouchers and why these were not available despite the incurrence of said obligation and recognition in the books.

We recommended and the City Accountant accounted for the actual labor payroll for JOs paid out of the obligated payables so that an adjusting entry could be made for the over/under-recorded amount. We further recommended that the City Accountant, henceforth, set an earlier deadline for the submission of documents for obligation at year-end and refuse those which do not comply with regulations so that it can also observe the deadline for the submission of the financial statements which is on February 14 of the ensuing year.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor, as representative of the City of Tanjay, request the respective financial treasurers of the League of Cities of the Philippines, Inc. and the Philippine Councilors League, Inc.-Negros Oriental Federation to acknowledge its payments (as well as collections from other members) with government Accountable Form No. 51 in order that the funds may be subjected to government accounting and auditing rules and regulations. We likewise recommended that the City withhold future payments until the leagues comply with this requirement.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Implemented
observations.reason: Official receipt from PCL has not been secured

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the use of the Toyota Hi-Ace ambulance, backhoe and excavator for ordinary, day-to-day operations be immediately stopped in order to preserve the utility of these vehicles to times of actual calamity solely for rescue and response activities in conformity with DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2014-155 dated December 17, 2014. Otherwise, if the LGU would wish to continue their present use, the LDRRMF should be reimbursed for the cost thereof.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Implemented
observations.reason: The ambulance and heavy equipment continued to be used; recommendation not implemented.

observations.recommendation: We required the officials concerned to restitute the amounts used for the purchase of high-end and expensive cell phones and tablet computers totaling P198,593.00.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Implemented
observations.reason: Awaiting Notices of Disallowance to be issued.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management initiate appropriate action to secure ownership of the Tanjay Water District to the extent possible pursuant to the terms of the MOA. Should the LGU decide otherwise, take steps to recover the amount it paid to LWUA on account of the TWD plus income earned from the time of its acquisition of the TWD in CY 2004 up to the present.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Implemented
observations.reason: Executive and Legislative branches of the LGU have yet to agree whether to take over the administration of the TWD or recover the amount paid to LWUA plus interest.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management immediately require the Kapuso’t Kapamilya Foundation, Inc. to submit a final Fund Utilization Report and Accomplishment Report together with the inspection report of the livelihood kits purchased conducted by an LGU representative, a certificate of project completion rendered by an LGU representative, list of beneficiaries with their acknowledgment of the kits received, duly verified by the internal auditor of the LGU. Inasmuch as the Notice of Disallowance issued was affirmed by the COA Regional Director, we further recommended that the amount disbursed be immediately refunded to the City.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Implemented
observations.reason: The disallowance is under appeal with the Commission Proper.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the immediate refund in full of the amounts received in excess of what is authorized in DBM Budget Circular No. 2010-3 totaling P9,141,000.00. Directed also the City Accountant and the City Budget Officer to be vigilant and not to certify disbursements which go beyond the provisions of the law.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Implemented
observations.reason: Appeal Memorandum was filed before the COA Regional Director No. VII.

observations.recommendation: We required the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to bill the COMELEC for the amount disbursed by the city as honoraria to the Board of Inspectors in the May 14, 2007 National and Local Elections, otherwise, require the recipients to refund the amount collected.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Implemented
observations.reason: Accountant allegedly does not have a complete set of documents needed to bill COMELEC. However, no request from COA has been made.

observations.recommendation: We required the accountable officers to file an application for relief of property accountability together with supporting documentation. In case of denial on the requests of relief, require them to pay the money value of such property.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Implemented
observations.reason: No action by the accountable officers.

observations.recommendation: We required the City Treasurer’s Office to lay down the requirements for the data reports that need to be generated by their office then require the supplier to install the programs for generating the specified computerized reports.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Implemented
observations.reason: No action taken to require the original contractor to perform his obligation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management secure the Transfer Certificate of Title to the lot purchased as an indispensable requirement, without prejudice to the price evaluation to be made based on the PAC report and BIR zonal valuations previously submitted by the City Assessor, including the necessity of incurring additional costs for the existing building thereon.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Implemented
observations.reason: No personnel assigned to undertake the necessary procedures to process the titling of said lot.

observations.recommendation: We required the City Accountant to maintain supplies ledger cards and the General Services Officer to maintain stock cards, respectively for all items subject to inventory as required in Sections 119 and 120 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not Implemented
observations.reason: Lack of personnel. Verification of records showed that inventory purchases were recorded as outright expenses contrary to Management’s assertion.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that the City Engineer assign
more personnel to assist in the preparation of the POW and its related duties to
accelerate the implementation of projects funded out of the 20% DF and to fast track
the backlogs in prior years in order to minimize idle funds and maximize the
usefulness of such projects. We further recommended that the City Engineer
enhance monitoring of project implementation by maintaining an updated list of
projects and taking corrective measures for projects that are lagging far behind
schedule. Any favorable action given hereto will ultimately redound to the benefit
of the city and its constituents, and to the achievement of the overall objective of the
government to make LGUs self-reliant communities.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a) Replenish the 20% DF from the General Fund Proper, through an
appropriation ordinance, the cost of administrative projects, e.g. landscaping
and park improvements, installation of elevator and the purchase of a
motorcycle and henceforth desist from appropriating the 20% DF for projects
that are not compliant with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1;
b) Realign the lump-sum appropriations for non-specific projects and anti-illegal
drugs campaign totaling ₱29,497,455.56 to specific development projects with
line by line allocation of inputs in a pre-determined location clearly indicated;
c) Submit thru the CDC and the LFC their evaluation of the projects whose
appropriations were reverted, with a categorical assessment as to its necessity
in order to guide us in the evaluation of similar projects which were equally
appropriated, as well as an explanation why the new items budgeted from the
reversions were not included in the local development plan for inclusion in the
annual investment plan, not just an addendum, given the extent of its financial
requirements;
d) Comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2013-004 in putting up
tarpaulins and signages for PPAs to assure the public of utmost transparency
and accountability in the implementation of projects; and
e) Closely monitor thru the City Engineering Office the completion of the Rizal
Park projects in accordance with specifications and within the timelines
stipulated in the contracts, notwithstanding the results of the technical
evaluation of the contracts.

We recommended and Management agreed to require:
a) The Inventory Committee and the CGSO to prioritize the reconciliation of
the RPCPPE with the accounting records, prepare the report for
submission to the Auditor and pinpoint persons responsible for the missing
properties, who should be made to pay the value thereof as shown in the
general ledger;
b) The CGSO to secure all copies of the ARE and update the filing thereof,
ensuring that these are principally considered especially during the
separation of accountable officers from the government service;
c) The CGSO to coordinate with the Accounting Office to fully account for
the CY 2018 PPE purchases, complete with data on the property tag, ARE,
accountable officer, and office/location of the equipment, otherwise, those
that cannot be accounted for shall be accordingly disallowed; and
d) The concerned department heads to prepare the Summaries of Supplies
and Materials Issued, otherwise a determination should be made as to who
should answer for the inventories which are no longer existent.

We recommended that Management:
a) Thru the City Accountant prepare a JEV to take up the necessary
correcting entries to eliminate the double recording of ₱6,450,166.00,
transfer ₱7,992.45 to the Watercrafts account, and reclassify
₱20,740,110.00 from Motor Vehicles to CHE;
b) Thru the GSO update its records to reconcile with the accounting records
and investigate the whereabouts of the equipment totaling ₱3,844,968.00
which were not found during inspection, submitting a written explanation
for the same;
c) Thru the CGSO and the City Engineer verify and submit documents for
the heavy equipment costing ₱750,000.00 and agricultural and forest
equipment costing ₱2,597,000.00 which were actually inspected but not
found in the books;
d) Thru the City Engineer maintain a separate logbook for each equipment
to monitor its use and ensure that the use thereof is for official purposes
duly authorized by the approving officials using a prescribed form, like the
Request for Issuance of Service Vehicle used by the Department of Public
Works and Highways, where the project name and duration of use is
clearly indicated, with supporting Driver’s Trip Ticket and the Monthly
Report of Travels; and
e) Thru the City Engineer install physical safeguards and controls to protect
equipment from loss, pilferage or unnecessary exposure to damage or
improper use, including the repair of those that are out of order, so that
maximum use thereof can be availed by the government.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a) Require the CHO to fully account for the medicines and medical supplies
purchased in CY 2018 so we may validate these with the records obtained
during the ocular inspection conducted and to serve as basis for adjusting
the corresponding inventory and expense accounts reported in the
financial statements;
b) Direct the BAC be fully aware of the terms in the ITB in order to act
accordingly in the evaluation of bids and strictly require bidders to
invariably indicate the brand names and to submit the CPR as an
indispensable requirement to provide assurance that the government
receives an equitable value for the expenditures incurred and explain the
incurrence of these deficiencies in the subject procurement;
c) Place upon the CHO the centralized accountability for all drugs and
medical supplies by maintaining the stock cards for each item, requiring
all departments, including barangays, to submit to the CHO their
corresponding RIS with logbooks and proof of distribution of the items
received with supporting documents, so that this could be consolidated
with the CHO’s own RIS to support the weekly or periodic SSMI to be
submitted to the Accounting Office for proper recording;
d) Institutionalize guidelines in the release of medicines to avoid any
ambiguity and promote fairness in the availment of such assistance from
the government; and
e) Ensure that mitigating or compensating controls like periodic
reconciliation of records and inventories, restricting release to qualified
recipients and with complete documentation, updated recording and
monitoring of consumption, are working effectively to reduce the risk of
error or irregularities.

We recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to prioritize the preparation
of the certified list of taxpayers and the taxes due by assigning personnel to update
the RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s index cards, a summary of which must be
submitted to the City Accountant as basis for setting up the SET receivables at the
beginning of the year in accordance with PPSAS 23 and Section 20 of the NGAS
Manual, Volume I, informing the team that a soft copy of the RPTAR covering CYs
2016 to 2018 will be furnished to the Accounting Office immediately in order to
update the previously recorded tax receivables.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a) Submit the requirements/documents necessary to substantiate the
procurement of the manlift in compliance with the Notice of Suspension that will be issued;
b) Ensure strict compliance to the provisions of the procurement law; and
c) Require the OIC-CGSO to submit a written explanation why the
equipment was not included in the inventory list for inspection and, with
the City Accountant, why there is a constant delay in the submission of
bidding documents to support the disbursement vouchers.

We recommended that Management:
a) consider terminating the contract, forfeiting the performance security,
deducting liquidated damages from whatever payment will be due to the
supplier, and imposing the sanctions for blacklisting per GPPB guidelines;
b) thru the City Accountant, prepare a correcting entry to recognize
inventory instead of expense for the supplies actually delivered;
c) direct the GSO to accept all deliveries before having them inspected by the
requesting units and to monitor completeness of deliveries with
corresponding measures to address erring suppliers; and
d) designate an inspector for each health unit who will be responsible for
checking the correctness of the medicines and supplies delivered to them.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a) Direct the City Accountant to send demand letters for the immediate
liquidation of unliquidated cash advances/secure a copy of the duly-
received transmittal letter for the liquidation of the intelligence funds,
withhold payment of any money due to the AO after due notice, and
henceforth, strictly observe the requirements/limitations stipulated under
Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-004;
b) Instruct the City Accountant to maintain updated index cards for cash
advances and submit the quarterly report with all required information on
or before the 10th day following the end of the quarter; and
c) Exhaust all means for the settlement of the accountability of AOs who are
already deceased or no longer in the service, and henceforth, ensure that
controls are in place so that all money and property accountability of
outgoing AOs are settled prior to the release of the clearance, otherwise the
joint liability clause under Section 104 of PD No. 1445 shall apply.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit the required documents
enumerated above, together with an explanation why it made a downpayment of more
than 50% of the contracted amount which is contrary to Section 6.10 of RA No. 10752,
and to secure the Transfer Certificate of Title to the lot purchased as an indispensable
requirement. We further recommended and the City Appraisal Committee likewise
agreed to submit documents to support its observance of the standards laid out in
Section 12 of RA No. 10752 in determining the market value of the lots purchased.

We recommended and Management agreed to comply with the maximum
employer’s share of the Pag-IBIG Fund premium of ₱100.00 per employee and
require employee-beneficiaries, including all officials who authorized the excessive
contributions, to refund the excess premiums to the government.

We recommended that the LDRRMC pass a resolution reverting the CYs 2011 and 2012 LDRRMF unspent balances to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund, for adoption by the Sangguniang Panlungsod. We further recommended that the members of the Local Finance Committee prepare the necessary transfer of funds and accounting reports to facilitate the said re appropriation and certify to the availability of said funds for subsequent re appropriation for social services.
observations.managementAction: AAR Already revered thru 2017 LDRRMC Resolution No. 002-04-2015 duly approved by Sangguniang Panlungsod

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant draw a Journal Entry Voucher recognizing the transfer of P11,895,108.72 from the General Fund to the Trust Fund using the accounts discussed in the foregoing paragraphs in compliance with the accounting requirements of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and to henceforth closely supervise accounting personnel in charge of said transfers.
observations.managementAction: AAR The City Accountant prepared JEV No. 300-18-06-006 dated June 26, 2018 recognizing the transfer of unutilized CY 2017 DRRMF from the GF to the TF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit the requirements/documents necessary to substantiate the procurement of the dump truck. Pending submission thereof, audit action on the foregoing transaction is hereby suspended.
observations.managementAction: AAR All documents relating to the purchase of a brand new dump truck have been submitted to COA Bais City.
observations.reason: Documents have not been evaluated yet.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct both the City Treasurer and the City Accountant to make representations with the depository bank to seek the necessary evidence/ documentation to support the deposit so that an adjusting entry could be made in the books to include the subject amount in the balance of the Cash in Bank account.
observations.managementAction: AAR Already deposited CY 2017 collections within the reglementary period. The PY balance pertained to unspent cash advances of the DO which were liquidated late due to difficulty in gathering all the DVs on time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer strictly observe the regulations governing receipt of government funds, seeing to that collections are remitted and deposited intact and on a daily basis or on the next banking day, whichever comes first, and to closely monitor personnel who may be delegated with such functions.
observations.managementAction: AAR Already deposited CY 2017 collections within the reglementary period. The PY balance pertained to unspent cash advances of the DO which were liquidated late due to difficulty in gathering all the DVs on time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a) Improve monitoring and reporting systems particularly in the preparation of the BRS to ensure that reconciling items that need attention are addressed on a timely basis so as not to defeat the purpose of preparing the said report; and b) Require the City Treasurer to secure all stale checks and their supporting documents, cancel the same and furnish the Accounting Office with the original DV for them to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher restoring the Cash in Bank and crediting Accounts Payable for stale checks less than two (2) years and Prior Years’ Adjustment for those beyond 2 years.
observations.managementAction: AAR Preparation of BRS already done regularly due to the hiring of qualified personnel in the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor, thru the Market Collectors Office, intensify collection of rentals due from the different delinquent stall owners by sending demand letters and by applying the remedies that are provided for under Sections 13 and 15.10 of the City of Tanjay Market Code of 2005 as amended. We further recommended that delinquent payments covered by compromise agreements be regularly updated to ensure that the terms set forth therein are diligently followed by the delinquent stall owner, otherwise demand full and immediate collection of the total delinquency.
observations.managementAction: AAR Ongoing collection of delinquent stall rentals based on a compromise agreement.
observations.reason: Not all have fully paid their delinquencies. Civil remedies to be undertaken in the fourth quarter of CY 2019 as agreed/planned.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous recommendation that management disapprove requests of leagues of elective officials for financial assistance where the project or activity to which it is to be used is not specified nor for the benefit of the public. We further recommended that the City Accountant prepare an adjusting entry to record the release of funds as a receivable subject to liquidation rather than as an outright expense, otherwise the same shall be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: AAR Journal Entry Voucher No. 100-19-04-0010 dated April 04, 2019 was prepared to record the receivable.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: a) concrete methods and procedures for the phaseout and the eventual closure of existing open dumps within a specific time frame, comply with the SCRP and provide adequate funding for the acquisition of an approved and compliant sanitary landfill.
observations.managementAction: AAR Implemented the Safe Closure and Rehabilitation Program and entered into a PPP Agreement with private contractor for the construction of sanitary landfill.
observations.reason: Sanitary landfill project is on-going.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to deduct the amounts already expended from the pertinent trust liability accounts and to credit the government equity account for the same amount. Henceforth, ensure strict compliance to the PPSAS in order to fairly present the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: AAR JEV No. GJ 300-18-06-008 dated 6/29/2018 was prepared.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the personnel concerned, particularly the signatories to the Individual Program of Work, the Abstract of Bids, canvass and Acceptance and Inspection Report, be made to answer for the alleged purchase of paints worth P250,000.00 and to refund such cost to the government. (Note: This recommendation appears possibly misaligned; original recommendation called for submission of requirements/documents pertinent to the importation of the two (2) units heavy equipment costing P20.424 million and that pending compliance thereto, audit action on this transaction is suspended.)
observations.managementAction: AAR Pending submission of documents. Procurement of goods is now done through public bidding except as those provided under alternative modes of procurement under RA No. 9184.
observations.reason: Documents have been submitted to COA Bais City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the RPCPPE be reconciled with the accounting records. We further recommended that the General Services Officer determine the persons accountable for the movable properties which could not be accounted for at the time of the physical count, who should be made to pay the value thereof as shown in the general ledger, and that the concerned department heads be required to prepare the Summaries of Supplies and Materials Issued, otherwise a determination should be made as to who should answer for the inventories which are no longer existent.
observations.managementAction: AAR Dicrepancies between the physical count reports and the book balances of property, plant and equipment and inventories has already been reconciled. The corresponding report has been submitted to the COA.
observations.reason: Verification of the said report showed items that were not found on hand but no subsequent action was taken therefor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the government appropriate the necessary amount for premiums and insure with the General Insurance Fund assets amounting to P161.9 million against any insurable risk such as fire, earthquake or other calamities in order that it can be compensated for any damages or losses thereof.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2016 allocated One Million Pesos (1M) for additional Insurance Premiums.
observations.reason: Not undertaken in CY 2018 and for updating of actual implementation in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the city Engineer prepare and submit to the City Accountant copies of the Certificates of Completion and Acceptance of the projects which have already been completed but are still carried in the CIP account, and for the latter to take up the appropriate journal entry reclassifying the cost of the projects from CIP to the appropriate asset account and to recognize depreciation on these assets in order to fairly present the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: AAR Not implemented. The City Engineer was requested to submit Certificates of Completion and Acceptance for the completed projects to facilitate transfer from CIP to the proper asset account.
observations.reason: No action taken by Management to transfer the CIP to the proper asset account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor give utmost attention and priority to the implementation of the projects outlined under the current and prior years’ 20% Development Fund, to determine the cause of the slow implementation thereof, and to institute corrective measures in order that desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes may be attained and for the constituents to enjoy the benefits therefrom.
observations.managementAction: AAR The 2016 AIP and Annual Budget of the City were declared Invalid and Inoperative by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan, hence the delay in the implementation of projects.
observations.reason: To conduct further inquiry into this matter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant be required to correct the affected accounts using the RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s index cards, a summary of which must be submitted by the City Treasurer to the former and to henceforth ensure that SET Receivables are recorded in the books at the beginning of the year.
observations.managementAction: AAR Updated records: SET Receivable was booked in CY 2016-2018 through adjusting entries. The City Treasurer submitted updated records for revision.
observations.reason: SET Receivable was booked in CY 2017 but not based on RPTARs; updated records were submitted in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor stop the deployment of job order employees to barangays and national government agencies and to consider the limiting or reducing the number of job order workers to be hired to what is necessary for the implementation of projects such that the wages of these laborers will be correctly charged to the projects being implemented.
observations.managementAction: AAR The creation of positions and the hiring of personnel to fill vacant positions is ongoing as well as the regularization of Job Order Workers assigned in the accounting office.
observations.reason: Number of JOs instead increased from 1,576 to 1,997 (an additional 421 JOs).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that in the preparation of its Annual Work and Financial Plan, the LCPC allocate more funds to PPAs responsive to the objective of providing protection to children against abuse, neglect, exploitation and violence and promotion of their rights and welfare as envisioned under RA No. 9344. Moreover, we recommended that it conduct the required quarterly meetings, maintain an updated database of children, prepare the required Development and Investment Plans for Children, and Local State of Children’s Report as required under DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120. Lastly, we recommended that the cost of the service vehicle be charged to the General Fund and reimbursed to the LCPC fund.
observations.managementAction: AAR The cost of the service vehicle was already reimbursed to the General Fund PPAs.
observations.reason: No priority given for the implementation of LCPC activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor assign to a specific person or persons the responsibility of assembling all contracts and their respective supporting documents and the submission thereof to COA within the reglementary period of five working days from perfection, using COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 as guide, as well as the preparation of a different set of supporting documents for the payment vouchers using COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 as reference.
observations.managementAction: AAR Contracts were already submitted to COA Bais City.
observations.reason: Contracts were submitted after the 5-day reglementary period and most were after release of funds, hence defeating the purpose of contract review prior to payment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor reconstitute the committee created to investigate the theft of unserviceable property, which should be required to submit its report as soon as possible, and to immediately initiate the filing of appropriate charges against Ms. Brenda B. Alama and city officials and personnel found to have connived and participated therein. We further recommended that the committee be reconstituted and the Disposal Committee, secretariat and technical staff undertake the disposal of unserviceable properties at the soonest time.
observations.managementAction: AAR Already issued Executive Order No. 24-07-2016 reconstituting the disposal committee.
observations.reason: The Disposal Committee is currently finalizing the inventory and physical count of unserviceable properties for disposal. However, no report has been prepared addressing that specific incident wherein properties were disposed without public auction. No accountabilities established even up to this time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the General Services Officer schedule a public auction as soon as possible in order that unserviceable properties can still command better prices.
observations.managementAction: AAR Unserviceable properties disposal is currently ongoing.
observations.reason: Public auction to follow once report is completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accountant assign additional personnel for the sorting and preparation of vouchers to be submitted to the auditor for post-audit and to monitor and ensure the timely preparation and submission of the quarterly trial balances and transaction documents within the reglementary periods.
observations.managementAction: AAR Regularization of Job Order Workers assigned in the accounting office is ongoing. Personnel assigned to pre-audit is also the same person assigned to segregate the documents for submission to COA.
observations.reason: The last quarter accounts for CY 2018 were not submitted as of the date of exit conference.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LDRRMF be reimbursed for the amount utilized for the purchase of office equipment, furniture and fixtures amounting to P37,375.00. Henceforth, limit the use of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund to the purposes allowed by law and regulations.
observations.managementAction: AAR Implementation: The amount was reimbursed.
observations.reason: No SP Resolution passed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the personnel concerned, particularly the signatories to the Individual Program of Work, the Abstract of Bids, canvass and Acceptance and Inspection Report, be made to answer for the alleged irregularities specifically the splitting of projects and lack of public bidding, and submit the following required supporting documents: i. Location plan for each project, specifying the structure/facility to be painted or improved; ii. Scope of work for each structure or facility; iii. Plans/ drawings; iv. Explanation for the absence of data in the labor component portion, except 'LGU'. Henceforth, consolidate procurement of similar materials needed on or about the same time and conduct public bidding to include major suppliers in Dumaguete City.
observations.managementAction: AAR Procurement of goods is now done through public bidding except as those provided under alternative modes of procurement under RA No. 9184.
observations.reason: Documents were submitted to the previous audit team and are still to be evaluated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the personnel concerned, particularly the signatories to the Individual Program of Work, the Abstract of Bids, canvass and Acceptance and Inspection Report, be made to answer for the alleged irregularities specifically the excessive quantities of office supplies purchased, the lack of public bidding, the participation of only four suppliers in each procurement, three of whom had no business establishment, was closed, and was not selling office supplies. In addition, submit proof of consumption of inks and toners by submitting Reports of Waste Materials together with the empty cartridges to the General Services Office for inspection by the auditor. Henceforth, procure office supplies through public bidding to be advertised in the websites of the PhilGEPS and the LGU as required by law and regulations and submit to COA a copy of all purchase orders.
observations.managementAction: AAR Procurement of goods is now done through public bidding except as those provided under alternative modes of procurement under RA No. 9184.
observations.reason: Some of the responsible officers are no longer in the service. Verification of records showed that inventory purchases were recorded as outright expenses contrary to Management’s assertion. Documents were submitted to the audit team and are still to be evaluated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the personnel concerned, particularly the signatories to the Individual Program of Work, the Abstract of Bids, canvass and Acceptance and Inspection Report, be made to answer for the alleged purchase of paints worth P250,000.00 and to refund such cost to the government.
observations.managementAction: AAR Procurement of goods is now done through public bidding except as those provided under alternative modes of procurement under RA No. 9184.
observations.reason: Documents for evaluation were submitted; verification showed the issue and evaluation is ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the government appropriate the necessary amount for premiums and insure with the General Insurance Fund assets amounting to P161.9 million against any insurable risk such as fire, earthquake or other calamities in order that it can be compensated for any damages or losses thereof.
observations.managementAction: None
observations.reason: The OIC-GSO was not able to identify the insurable properties, hence they were also not able to submit the list for assessment and computation of the insurance premium which would have served as basis for the appropriation to be sought.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Engineer prepare and submit to the City Accountant copies of the Certificates of Completion and Acceptance of the projects which have already been completed but are still carried in the CIP account, and for the latter to take up the appropriate journal entry reclassifying the cost of the projects from CIP to the appropriate asset account and to recognize depreciation on these assets in order to fairly present the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: None
observations.reason: The City Engineer overlooked the preparation of the certificates of completion.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that in the preparation of its Annual Work and Financial Plan, the LCPC allocate more funds to PPAs responsive to the objective of providing protection to children against abuse, neglect, exploitation and violence and promotion of their rights and welfare as envisioned under R.A. 9344. Moreover, we recommended that it conduct the required quarterly meetings, maintain an updated database of children, prepare the required Development and Investment Plans for Children, and Local State of Children’s Report as required under DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120. Lastly, we recommended that the cost of the service vehicle be charged to the General Fund and reimbursed to the LCPC fund.
observations.managementAction: No comment was given by Management.
observations.reason: No action.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor reconstitute the committee created to investigate the theft of unserviceable property, which should be required to submit its report as soon as possible, and to immediately initiate the filing of appropriate charges against Ms. Brenda B. observations.managementAction: No action indicated

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the RPCPPE be reconciled with the accounting records.
We further recommended that the General Services Officer determine the persons
accountable for the movable properties which could not be accounted for at the time of
the physical count, who should be made to pay the value thereof as shown in the
general ledger, and that the concerned department heads be required to prepare the
Summaries of Supplies and Materials Issued, otherwise a determination should be
made as to who should answer for the inventories which are no longer existent.

We recommended that Management submit all the requirements/documents
pertinent to the importation of the two units heavy equipment costing P20.424 million
in compliance with the Notice of Suspension that will be issued.

We recommended that Management submit the requirements/documents
necessary to substantiate the procurement of the dump truck in compliance with the
Notice of Suspension that will be issued.

We recommended that the City Treasurer strictly observe the regulations
governing receipt of government funds, seeing to it that collections are remitted and
deposited intact and on a daily basis or on the next banking day, whichever comes first,
and to closely monitor personnel who may be delegated with such functions.

We recommended that the City Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting
entries to deduct the amounts already expended from the pertinent trust liability
accounts and to credit the government equity account through the use of prior period
adjustment for the same amount. Henceforth, ensure strict compliance to the PPSAS
in order to fairly present the accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Treasurer, thru the Market Collectors Office,
intensify collection of rentals due from the different delinquent stall owners by sending
demand letters and by applying the remedies that are provided for under Sections 13
and 15.10 of the City of Tanjay Market Code of 2005 as amended. We further
recommended that delinquent payments covered by compromise agreements be
regularly updated to ensure that the terms set forth therein are diligently followed by
the delinquent stall owner, otherwise demand full and immediate collection of the total
delinquency.

We recommended that Management instruct both the City Treasurer and the
City Accountant to make representations with the depository bank to seek the necessary
evidence/ documentation to support the deposit so that an adjusting entry could be
made in the books to include the subject amount in the balance of the Cash in Bank
account.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor, as representative of the
City of Tanjay, request the respective treasurers of the League of Cities of the
Philippines, Inc. and the Philippine Councilors League, Inc.-Negros Oriental
Federation to acknowledge its payments (as well as collections from other members)
with government Accountable Form No. 51 in order that the funds may be subjected to
government accounting and auditing rules and regulations. We likewise
recommended that the City withhold future payments until the leagues comply with this
requirement.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that management disapprove
requests of leagues of elective officials for financial assistance where the project or
activity to which it is to be used is not specified nor for the benefit of the public. We
further recommended that the City Accountant prepare an adjusting entry to record the
release of funds as a receivable subject to liquidation rather than as an outright
expense, otherwise the same shall be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management:
a) Improve monitoring and reporting systems particularly in the preparation of
the BRS to ensure that reconciling items that need attention are addressed
on a timely basis so as not to defeat the purpose of preparing the said report;
and
b) Require the City Treasurer to secure all stale checks and their supporting
documents, cancel the same and furnish the Accounting Office with the
original DV for them to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher restoring the
Cash in Bank and crediting Accounts Payable for stale checks less than two
(2) years and Prior Years’ Adjustment for those beyond 2 years.

We recommended that Management concretize methods and procedures for the
phaseout and the eventual closure of existing open dumps within a specific time frame,
comply with the SCRP and provide adequate funding for the acquisition of an
approved and compliant sanitary landfill.

We recommended that the OIC GSO and the inventory team prepare corrected RPCPPE and RPCI as of December 31, 2016, to complete the inventory by reconciling the count with the corresponding accounts in the books, to investigate variances and to charge the value of unaccounted properties to personnel accountable therefor.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-GSO already corrected the report format of the RPCPPE. They still maintained the first report for ease of reference.
observations.reason: The report was not reconciled with accounting records due to lack of technical personnel and the existence of prior year records which could not be traced to the person accountable.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor give utmost attention and priority to the implementation of the projects outlined under the current and prior years’ 20% Development Fund, to determine the cause of the slow implementation thereof, and to institute corrective measures in order that desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes may be attained and for the constituents to enjoy the benefits therefrom.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-GSO already implemented especially after the 20% transition from the previous administration.
observations.reason: The appropriations could not be fully implemented coupled with new appropriations which could not be easily coped with nor fully implemented immediately.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant be required to correct the affected accounts using the RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s index cards, a summary of which must be submitted by the City Treasurer to the former and to henceforth ensure that Receivables are recorded in the books at the beginning of the year.
observations.managementAction: No action indicated
observations.reason: The OIC indicated that they started recognizing SET Receivable starting CY 2018. However, prior years’ uncollected balances were not recognized as receivables.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant prepare a journal entry voucher to recognize the acquisition of heavy equipment, and to adjust the Other Receivables account by the amount of P20,270,642.85. We further recommended that depreciation expense be recognized on the said equipment effective the following month after delivery.
observations.managementAction: Already prepared GJ JEV No. 100-17-08-0034 dated 9/8/17 to recognize the equipment in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that henceforth, the grant of financial assistance under the DSWD program AICS be limited solely to the situations enumerated under DSWD Circular No. 02, series of 2014 to the exclusion of cash gifts to senior citizens celebrating their birthdays. Grant of cash benefits to senior citizens should conform to the law, which is a death benefit of not less than P2,000.00 and social pension to indigent senior citizens not exceeding P500.00 per month. We further recommended that the GIS be diligently prepared by social workers in order that the grant of financial assistance will be justified.
observations.managementAction: All recipients were absorbed by the LGU, with positions created to accommodate those charged to the SEF. The CY 2017 budget already included an appropriation for the construction of school buildings.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor stop the deployment of job order employees to barangays and national government agencies and to consider limiting or reducing the number of job order workers to be hired to what is necessary for the implementation of projects such that the wages of these laborers will be correctly charged to the projects being implemented.
observations.managementAction: The city already stopped the deployment of JOs to barangays and NGAs. JOs were reduced from 1,723 to 1,576.
observations.reason: Verification of the report submitted by the HRMO still showed that there are JOs charged to projects despite being office-based.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the use of the Toyota Hi-Ace ambulance, backhoe and excavator for ordinary, day-to-day operations be immediately stopped in order to preserve the utility of these vehicles to times of actual calamity, in conformity with DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2014-155 dated December 17, 2014. Otherwise, if the LGU would wish to continue their present use, the LDRRMF should be reimbursed for the cost thereof.
observations.managementAction: No further appropriations for said activities were included in the annual budget, except for the provision of P1,500/employee for cultural activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that henceforth, management disapprove requests of leagues of elective officials for financial assistance where the project or activity to which it is to be used is not specified nor for the benefit of the public. We further recommended that the City Accountant prepare an adjusting entry to record the release of funds as a receivable subject to liquidation rather than as an outright expense, otherwise the same shall be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant stated that he will prepare the adjusting entry as well as send a demand to PCL for the liquidation of the city funds given as financial assistance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor, as representative of the City of Tanjay, request the respective treasurers of the League of Cities of the Philippines and the Philippine Councilors League-Negros Oriental Federation to acknowledge its payments (as well as collections from other members) with government Accountable Form No. 51 in order that the funds may be subjected to government accounting and auditing rules and regulations. We likewise recommended that the City withhold future payments until the leagues comply with this requirement.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor promised to write a letter to the LCP to secure the required official receipt. Management was likewise amenable to the recommendation to withhold future payments unless the leagues acknowledge their payments with the proper receipt.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor assign to a specific person or persons responsibility of assembling all the contracts and their respective supporting documents and the submission thereof to COA within the reglementary period of five working days from perfection, using COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 as guide, as well as the preparation of a different set of supporting documents for the payment vouchers using COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 as reference.
observations.managementAction: Some of the contracts were already submitted.
observations.reason: The OIC-GSO who has a voluminous workload is the same person responsible for assembling all contracts and their respective supporting documents. There is no other personnel in his office that he can rely on to do the job.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the utilization of the SEF for purposes not allowed by law be stopped and that the current erroneous practice of transferring funds to the SEF to cover the compensation of employees who are not appointees of the city be corrected. Representations may be made with the DepEd for the absorption of the secondary school teachers and watchmen while other construction of school buildings should utilize the SEF primarily for its intended purpose.
observations.managementAction: All personnel were absorbed by the LGU, with positions created to accommodate those charged to the SEF. The CY 2017 budget already included an appropriation for the construction of school buildings.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management, henceforth, exercise caution in the allocation and expenditure of public funds, particularly, for fiesta and Christmas activities where GAA provision of entertainment is the major consideration, ensuring that regulations are observed and irregular, unnecessary and excessive uses of public funds are prevented.
observations.managementAction: The City already observed the P5,000 minimum pay (based on the latest GAA) for new certifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant’s Office be more careful in the issuance of certifications on the net take home pay of an employee, making sure that the P3,000.00 minimum net pay is observed. In cases where the net take home pay is reduced to amounts below the minimum, deductions shall be prioritized in the order of preference stated in the Annual GAA to comply with the net take home pay of at least P3,000.00. Henceforth, utilize the SEF primarily for its intended purpose.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant has been requiring employees granted cash advances for travel to liquidate within seven (7) days upon return to station, otherwise automatic payroll deduction will be effected.
observations.reason: There are still those with existing loan obligations that still fall below the minimum net take home pay requirement. These shall eventually increase once their loans are fully paid.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the General Services Officer to schedule a public auction as soon as possible in order that unserviceable properties can still command better prices.
observations.managementAction: A public auction was scheduled in September 2015 but covering only a portion of the unserviceable properties amounting to P734,160.00. The auction did not push through but the items were sold to a private buyer (please see related observation).
observations.reason: The General Services Office does not have the required manpower.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant and the heads of Departments enjoin all officials and employees to observe COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 89 of P.D. 1445 and to enforce the appropriate administrative and/or legal sanctions provided under said COA circular. Demand letters are also sent regularly.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant has been requiring employees granted cash advances for travel to liquidate within seven (7) days upon return to station, otherwise automatic payroll deduction will be effected. Demand letters are also sent regularly.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the city government appropriate the necessary
amount for premiums and insure with the General Insurance Fund assets amounting to P161.9
million against any insurable risk such as fire, earthquake or other calamities in order that it can be
compensated for any damages or losses thereof.

We recommended that the OIC GSO and the inventory team prepare corrected
RPCPPE and RPCI as of December 31, 2016, to complete the inventory by reconciling the count
with the corresponding accounts in the books, to investigate variances and to charge the value of
unaccounted properties to personnel accountable therefor.

We recommended that the City Engineer prepare and submit to the City
Accountant copies of the Certificates of Completion and Acceptance of the projects which have
already been completed but are still carried in the CIP account, and for the latter to take up the
appropriate journal entry reclassifying the cost of the projects from CIP to the appropriate asset
account and to recognize depreciation on these assets in order to fairly present the accounts in the
financial statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor give utmost attention and priority to the
implementation of the projects outlined under the current and prior years’ 20% Development Fund,
to determine the cause of the slow implementation thereof, and to institute corrective measures in
order that desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes may be
attained and for the constituents to enjoy the benefits therefrom.

We recommended that the City Accountant be required to correct the affected
accounts using the RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s index cards, a summary of which must be
submitted by the City Treasurer to the former and to henceforth ensure that SET Receivables are
recorded in the books at the beginning of the year.

We recommended that the City Accountant prepare a journal entry voucher to
recognize the acquisition of heavy equipment, and to adjust the Other Receivables account by the
amount of P20,270,642.85. We further recommended that depreciation expense be recognized on the
said equipment effective the following month after delivery.

We recommended that henceforth, the grant of financial assistance under the
DSWD program AICS be limited solely to the situations enumerated under DSWD Circular No. 02,
series of 2014 to the exclusion of cash gifts to senior citizens celebrating their birthdays. Grant of cash
benefits to senior citizens should conform to the law, which is a death benefit of not less than P2,000.00
and social pension to indigent senior citizens not exceeding P500.00 per month. We further
recommended that the GIS be diligently prepared by social workers in order that the grant of financial
assistance will be justified.

We recommended that the City Mayor stop the deployment of job order employees
to barangays and national government agencies and to consider limiting or reducing the number of job
order workers to be hired to what is necessary for the implementation of projects such that the wages
of these laborers will be correctly charged to the projects being implemented.

We recommended that in the preparation of its Annual Work and Financial Plan,
the LCPC allocate more funds to PPAs responsive to the objective of providing protection to children
against abuse, neglect, exploitation and violence and promotion of their rights and welfare as envisioned
under R.A. 9344. Moreover, we recommended that it conduct the required quarterly meetings, maintain
an updated database of children, prepare the required Development and Investment Plans for Children,
and Local State of Children’s Report as required under DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120.
Lastly, we recommended that the cost of the service vehicle be charged to the General Fund and
reimbursed to the LCPC fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor assign to a specific person or persons the responsibility of assembling all contracts and their respective supporting documents and the submission thereof to COA within the reglementary period of five working days from perfection, using COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 as guide, as well as the reference.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2015 Some of the contracts were already submitted.
observations.reason: The OIC-GSO who has a voluminous workload is the same person responsible of assembling all contracts and their respective supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant refuse to process payrolls unless supported by the DTRs with applications for leave, office/travel orders, and certificates of appearance/attendance of all employees listed in the payroll. We recommended further that the Paymaster/Disbursing Officer see to it that claims by persons other than payees are supported by written authorization from the payee concerned, and that the authorized person is an immediate member of the family or the liaison officer of the office.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2015 payrolls were processed previously without proper supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor reconstitute the committee created to investigate the theft of unserviceable property, which should be required to submit its report as soon as possible, and to immediately initiate the filing of appropriate charges against Ms. Brenda B. Alama and city officials and personnel found to have connived and participated therein.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2015 contracts were already submitted.
observations.reason: No specific reason.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the utilization of the SEF for purposes not allowed by law be stopped and that the current erroneous practice of transferring funds to the SEF to cover the budget of personnel who are not appointees of the city be corrected. Representations may be made with the DepEd for the absorption of the secondary school teachers and watchmen while other non-teaching personnel may be charged to the General Fund. Henceforth, utilize the SEF primarily for its intended purpose.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2015 Salaries of school watchmen were charged to the General Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the exercise of caution in the allocation and expenditure of public funds, particularly, in the allocation and expenditure for fiesta and christmas activities where entertainment is the major consideration, ensuring that regulations are observed and irregular, unnecessary and excessive uses of public funds are prevented.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2015 The city appropriated P6,500,000.00 and P4,244,564.50 of expenses are necessary since it is already part of the culture.
observations.reason: Management believes that such expenses are necessary since it is already part of the culture.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the rules and regulations on the use of government motor vehicles be strictly implemented by requiring for each and every trip a trip ticket which contains all the required information before it is approved, by limiting vehicle use to official purposes only, by assigning personnel to monitor the reasonableness of fuel consumption, by ensuring that the odometers of all vehicles are working properly, and by requiring passengers to sign the certification as to official use. In addition, we recommended the correction of the trip ticket form to conform to the prescribed format (Annex 21).
observations.managementAction: AAR 2015 The accounting office strictly requires the trip tickets to be properly accomplished.
